title: Strategic CSR Communication: A Comparative Study of Legitimation in Chinese and American Energy Companies
authors: Changhong Li, Yingqui Wang
abstract: Background: While corporate social responsibility (CSR) reporting has been widely studied, fewer works have systematically examined how legitimation strategies are discursively constructed across generic structure in energy-sector disclosures. Literature review: Prior research has explored microlinguistic features and move structures, but little attention has been paid to how rhetorical strategies of legitimation are embedded across generic stages. Research questions: 1. How are the five major legitimation strategies linguistically realized across the move-step structures of Chinese and American energy CSR reports? 2. How do legitimation strategies co-occur with different discourse types of Chinese and American energy CSR reports? 3. How do the external factors influence the selection of legitimation strategies in CSR reporting practices? Methodology: This study analyzes 40 CSR reports (2020-2023) from Chinese and American energy companies using a corpus-assisted and move-step-based approach that integrates critical genre analysis and an integrated analytical legitimation framework. Lexical bundles and grammatical features are annotated across generic stages and discourse types to identify legitimation strategies and trace how they vary by national context and external pressures. Results and conclusions: This study identifies eight moves and 13 steps—including a newly observed move, M8: Elaboration of performance in the public health crisis—as well as four discourse types. Chinese reports emphasize authorization and moral narrative, while American reports favor rationalization, analogies of moralization and cosmos narrative. These strategies are embedded across moves and discourse types, adapting to institutional logics and external pressures.

title: A New High-Ratio-Transformer Based Soft Open Point with Mixed-Frequency Modulation Method
authors: Haixi Zhao, Jianxi Lan, Wu Chen, Yueyin Wang, Yutong Chen, Yubo Yuan
abstract: This paper presents a new high-ratio-transformer based soft open point (HRT-SOP) topology. By adopting mixed-frequency modulation method, medium-frequency circulating currents are injected into the proposed HRT-SOP, and two circulating current paths are constructed to balance the submodule dc voltage. The use of high-ratio transformer, which step down the medium voltage directly to the low voltage, allows for a significant reduction in the number of submodules. The circulating current models are derived, the control strategies are described and the key parameters are designed. The proposed HRT-SOP is compared with other conventional and state-of-the-art SOP topologies. The comparison shows that the proposed SOP requires least number of semiconductors and capacitors and therefore has the lowest cost. Finally, the correctness and validity of the proposed HRT-SOP are verified by simulation and experimental results.

title: Bridging Enablers and Barriers in E-Government Adoption: A Systematic Literature Review
authors: Beny Prasetyo, Siti Rochimah, Tony Dwi Susanto
abstract: E-government adoption is affected by a complex confluence of technical, organizational, and social challenges, despite the rapid pace of digital transformation in the public sector. Therefore, a systematic analysis of 68 selected studies carried out between 2020 and 2025 was addressed to monitor the changing landscape of e-government adoption. The outcome of the analysis focused on the theoretical deviation from single model methods toward integrated frameworks, reflecting a convergence of quality-based and behavioral models. The results showed that adoption focused on infrastructure availability, pragmatic utility, technological trust, and human capital readiness. Additionally, security risks and structural deficits remained the main barriers. To offer a comprehensive understanding, both enablers and barriers were mapped into the Technology-Organization-Environment-Individual (TOE) framework, and this entailed the inclusion of the Individual dimension. The aim was to map human-related factors, particularly digital literacy, computer self-efficacy as well as individual resistance and other determinants not captured in the traditional TOE. To reduce the diverse barriers, this study harmonized evidence-based strategies, for example, offline-first architectures and tiered competency training. The insights led to the production of a generic map for policymakers to ensure that the disparity between the potential and reality of technology adoption in e-government ecosystems was bridged.

title: On the Object Recognition Via Free-Fall Impact Vibration Signals
authors: Chin-Chieh Chang, Van-Thuan Tran, Jung-Tsung Kuo, Wei-Ho Tsai
abstract: Object recognition through physical interaction provides an alternative sensing approach for robotic systems and intelligent devices when visual information is unavailable or insufficient. This paper investigates object recognition using free-fall impact vibration signals. We collect vibration data using an inertial sensor module measuring acceleration and angular velocity during object impacts on a spring-mounted wooden platform, and evaluate our approach on five datasets with increasing difficulty: 10 objects with distinct weights (Set 1), 15 objects with similar weights but different shapes (Set 2), 20 highly similar screwdriver bits (Set 3), a unified 45-object dataset, and an extended 60-object dataset. We propose a Temporal Convolutional Network (TCN) architecture and examine various approaches to process acceleration and angular velocity signals along the vertical axis. Session-based 10-fold cross-validation, where each fold corresponds to a complete data collection session, is used to ensure evaluation of real-world generalization capability. Experimental results demonstrate that TCN significantly outperforms baseline architectures (1D-CNN, CNN-LSTM, SVM) across all datasets and experiments. On the challenging unified dataset, single-sensor TCN achieves 94.27% accuracy with angular velocity data, while dual-sensor fusion achieves 94.51%. TCN maintains robust performance from Set 1 to Set 3, demonstrating practical applicability even with highly similar objects. Remarkably, TCN achieves this performance with the parameter efficiency (0.6M parameters for single-branch, 1.2M for dual-branch), outperforming 1D-CNN dual-branch (8.3M parameters, 92.80% accuracy) with much fewer parameters. Analysis also reveals that angular velocity consistently outperforms acceleration by 1.71% to 15.69% depending on the model and validates the effectiveness of TCN for impact vibration classification with moderate training data. The method demonstrates robustness across signal lengths (1-2s optimal) and maintains low cross-session variance (1-2%), indicating stable performance across environmental conditions.

title: A Hierarchical Deep Reinforcement Learning Model for Joint Optimization of Urban Traffic Congestion and Cost in Intelligent Transportation Systems
authors: Zixin Zhu
abstract: Urban traffic congestion poses a persistent challenge to modern cities, resulting in increased travel delay, energy consumption, and economic loss. As a critical problem in intelligent transportation systems, urban congestion management requires coordinated and adaptive decision-making across both supply-side and demand-side control mechanisms. Existing traffic management approaches usually address congestion from either the supply side through traffic signal control or the demand side via congestion pricing, while their intrinsic interactions and multi-scale coordination remain insufficiently explored. To overcome these challenges, this paper proposes a hierarchical deep reinforcement learning framework, termed JCPO-DRL, for joint congestion and pricing optimization in urban traffic networks. The framework decomposes the joint control task into a two-layer decision process, where an upper-layer policy regulates network-level congestion pricing at a coarse temporal scale and a lower-layer policy performs adaptive traffic signal control conditioned on pricing decisions, enabling coordinated supply–demand regulation while avoiding the curse of dimensionality. Extensive experiments under diverse traffic scenarios show that JCPO-DRL consistently outperforms state-of-the-art signal-only and pricing-only baselines, reducing average travel delay by up to 19% and improving traffic throughput by up to 4% compared with representative baselines, and maintaining moderate congestion pricing cost. These results demonstrate the effectiveness of hierarchical joint optimization and highlight the potential of the proposed framework for intelligent urban traffic management in intelligent transportation systems.

title: CPUL: An Effective Cross-Social Platform User Linkage for Location Imbalance
authors: Ruiting Liu, Wenqi Shi, Ruixiang Li, Bing Zhang, Xiangyang Luo
publish: IEEE Transactions on Computational Social Systems
abstract: Cross-platform user linkage can connect the accounts of a user on different social platforms. User linkage across platforms based on location is a typical method in current research. These methods typically rely on direct trajectory matching (such as the shortest spatial distance or cooccurrence frequency of location pairs) to measure user similarity, while ignoring the potential influence of locations beyond these direct associations. When locations across platforms are unbalanced, the numerous locations on the data-rich platform are overlooked, reducing the accuracy of user linkage. This manuscript proposes an effective user linkage method for imbalanced location data across social platforms. First, a query likelihood model (QLM) is introduced to model the location distribution correlation between candidate users and target users, to fully utilize location information. Next, the map is gridded and the inverse user geographical frequency, which highlights significant locations with prominent user features, is integrated into the QLM to more precisely assess correlations between users. Finally, user mobility pattern similarity is measured using spatial correlation, and user similarity is calculated by combining it with query likelihood probability. The candidate user with the highest similarity is selected to complete the linkage with the target user. Experimental results on GeoWS dataset and four public datasets (Geolife, GeoHalf, GeoThin, GeoSparse) show that the proposed method outperforms six existing typical methods (e.g., <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$k$</tex-math> </inline-formula>-BCT, GS) in average user linkage accuracy across 10 grid cell partitioning schemes. At 50-m grid granularity, accuracy improves by an average of 10.04%, 24.86%, 8.19%, 4.34%, 16.57%, and 15.53%, respectively.

title: IEEE Robotics and Automation Practice Publication Information
authors: N/A

title: Are Deep Learning Models Trained on Synthetic Data Actually Reliable in InSAR? A Self-Supervised Learning Approach for InSAR Phase Denoising
authors: Kang Liang, Zhong Lu, Jinwoo Kim
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: Deep learning is revolutionizing the way InSAR data are processed. Supervised deep learning models trained on synthetic datasets have demonstrated strong performance according to conventional evaluation metrics. However, in practice, many supervised InSAR phase denoising filters tend to oversmooth fringe patterns and generate ghost fringes in decorrelated regions, raising concerns about the reliability of models trained solely on synthetic data in real-world applications. In this work, we introduce Noise2Fringe, an unsupervised learning framework that enables the direct training of denoising filters on noisy interferograms, eliminating the need for clean references, while achieving performance comparable to that of supervised models. This framework allows a fair comparison between models trained exclusively on real versus synthetic data, independent of the training scheme. On synthetic benchmarks, models trained on real data appear to underperform compared to those trained on synthetic data. However, in real-world interferograms, the opposite holds: synthetic-trained models oversmooth and generate ghost fringes, whereas real-trained models avoid these artifacts. These findings reveal (i) a much larger gap between synthetic and real data than previously assumed, (ii) an overestimation of the effectiveness of synthetic-trained models, and (iii) deficiencies in current evaluation methods that rely heavily on simulated benchmarks. We further find that incorporating auxiliary information, such as the Amplitude Dispersion Index, into the input can enhance denoising performance. Overall, this study demonstrates that models trained solely on synthetic data may be far less reliable in real-world applications than suggested by their benchmark results, and emphasizes that generating more realistic training datasets should take precedence over designing increasingly complex model architectures.

title: Power Converters for Green Hydrogen Production: An Application-Oriented Review
authors: Tayebeh Faghihi, Shadi Khodakaramzadeh, Pavol Bauer, Hani Vahedi
abstract: The decarbonization of energy systems has positioned green hydrogen as a pivotal energy carrier, with water electrolysis emerging as the most mature and scalable technology for sustainable hydrogen production. When powered by renewable sources such as solar photovoltaics (PVs) and wind, electrolyzers enable a carbon-neutral pathway while requiring advanced power conversion systems. This article presents an application-oriented review of power electronic converter technologies for renewable-powered water electrolysis, based on reported studies and commercially implemented systems. The discussion begins with an overview of the main types of electrolyzers and their distinct electrical and dynamic characteristics. Renewable energy supply characteristics are then examined, highlighting intermittency, variability, and integration challenges associated with solar, wind, and hybrid sources. Technical requirements for power converters, such as efficiency and cost, reliability, voltage and current regulation, power quality, scalability, system complexity, and isolation, are reviewed based on literature and commercial systems. The review further surveys AC–DC rectifier topologies, from diode-based designs to advanced controlled and multilevel configurations, as well as nonisolated and isolated DC–DC converters. Practical design considerations and topology selection strategies for large-scale electrolyzer plants are discussed, with reference to existing hydrogen production facilities and commercial power converter solutions. This review provides a consolidated reference for researchers and engineers, bridging electrolyzer operational requirements with state-of-the-art power electronics solutions for renewable hydrogen generation.

title: Scalable Hardware–Software Co-Verification through Linux Driver Reuse: Methodology Validated on USB4 and xHCI Host Controllers.
authors: Suchir Gupta, Amit Sharma, Suneetha Suryadevara, Vishnuvardhan Reddy
publish: 2026 27th International Symposium on Quality Electronic Design (ISQED)
abstract: Modern SoC verification demands robust hardware/software co-verification strategies to ensure functional correctness and protocol compliance. Bare-metal device drivers serve as critical verification components for IP-level testbenches, hardware/software co-verification, and pre-silicon validation. However, developing verification-grade bare-metal drivers from scratch is resource-intensive, error-prone, and delays verification closure. This paper introduces a systematic methodology for transforming production-grade Linux drivers into bare-metal verification drivers using SystemVerilog DPI-C interfaces. Key innovations include: (1) a hardware abstraction layer with DPI-C interfaces bridging software and RTL, (2) a structured driver transformation process preserving original semantics while ensuring traceability, and (3) a multi-level validation strategy spanning simulation and emulation platforms to guarantee functional correctness. We validated the framework on Universal Serial Bus 4 (USB4) Connection Manager and USB Extensible Host Controller interface (xHCI) drivers, achieving an $80 \%$ reduction in driver development time while reusing 39K–45K lines of production-validated Linux code. All functional tests passed confirming cycle-accurate behavior and complete equivalence with Linux implementations across both simulation and emulation environments. This methodology enables accelerated verification closure, improved stimulus realism, and reduced risk across diverse hardware platforms. The framework is broadly applicable to any Linux device driver, enabling systematic reuse of mature software for pre-silicon validation.

title: GRPO with State Mutations: Improving LLM-Based Hardware Test Plan Generation
authors: Dimple Vijay Kochar, Nathaniel Pinckney, Guan-Ting Liu, Chia-Tung Ho, Chenhui Deng, Haoxing Ren, Brucek Khailany
publish: 2026 27th International Symposium on Quality Electronic Design (ISQED)
abstract: RTL design often relies heavily on ad-hoc testbench creation early in the design cycle. While large language models (LLMs) show promise for RTL code generation, their ability to reason about hardware specifications and generate targeted test plans remains largely unexplored. We present the first systematic study of LLM reasoning capabilities for RTL verification stimuli generation, establishing a two-stage framework that decomposes test plan generation from testbench execution. Our benchmark reveals that state-of-the-art models, including DeepSeek-R1 and Claude-4.0-Sonnet, achieve only 15.7$21.7 \%$ success rates on generating stimuli that pass golden RTL designs. To improve LLM-generated stimuli, we develop a comprehensive training methodology combining supervised finetuning with a novel reinforcement learning approach, GRPO with State Mutation (GRPO-SMu), which enhances exploration by varying input mutations. Our approach leverages a treebased branching mutation strategy to construct training data comprising equivalent and mutated trees, moving beyond linear mutation approaches to provide rich learning signals. Training on this curated dataset, our 7B parameter model achieves a 33.3 % golden test pass rate and a 13.9 % mutation detection rate, representing a $17.6 \%$ absolute improvement over baseline and outperforming much larger general-purpose models. These results demonstrate that specialized training methodologies can significantly enhance LLM reasoning capabilities for hardware verification tasks, establishing a foundation for automated subunit testing in semiconductor design workflows.

title: Hardware Software Co-Optimization for RISC-V Based High-Performance Hyperdimensional Computing Architectures
authors: Priyanka Agarwal, M Arun, Kumar N S Chandan, Shrinidhi S Rao, Madhav Rao
publish: 2026 27th International Symposium on Quality Electronic Design (ISQED)
abstract: Hyperdimensional Computing (HDC) is a brain-inspired learning paradigm that encodes information in high-dimensional vectors, enabling lightweight and efficient classification. While such representations improve accuracy and robustness, they also demand high computational, storage, and energy costs. To overcome these challenges, we propose a hardwareoptimized RISC-V co-processor architecture for efficient HDC execution in low-power, resource-constrained systems. The coprocessor leverages a custom instruction designed explicitly for HDC and integrates it using a Single Instruction, Multiple Data (SIMD) approach to enable parallelism and improve throughput. Furthermore, we present a software optimization scheme in the form of a genetic algorithm-based pruning framework that efficiently reduces the feature dimensionality, significantly lowering the computational burden on the co-processor with negligible accuracy loss. FPGA implementation results indicate notable gains, delivering a $16.44 \times$ improvement in energy efficiency and a $21.57 \times$ boost in throughput compared to the RISC-V cores, while achieving $9 \%$ savings in resource utilization and up to $24.78 \%$ reduction in power consumption relative to existing state-of-the-art (SOTA) designs. Additionally, our approach achieves a $65 \%$ reduction in the total number of clock cycles relative to SOTA designs. ASIC validation further confirms the benefits, showing upto 15.89% savings in silicon footprint, a 12.33% reduction in power consumption, while maintaining a comparable critical path delay relative to the baseline architecture. All design files are made freely available for further use by the designers and research community.

title: Characterization of the Stability of DRAM Read Disturbances
authors: Roberto Capoferri, Alessandro Barenghi, Luca Breveglieri, Khalil Gammoh, Niccolò Izzo, Gerardo Pelosi
publish: 2026 27th International Symposium on Quality Electronic Design (ISQED)
abstract: Read disturbance phenomena, such as RowHammer and RowPress, are well-known causes for data corruption in DRAM memories, which have been shown to constitute exploitable security vulnerabilities. Indeed, both phenomena alter the memory locations nearby a row that is targeted by an attacker, potentially leading to writing data in memory locations that should not be accessible by her. In this work, we take a constructive point of view on the read disturbance phenomena. Observing that the data alteration patterns depend on the single device instance, we characterize them with the intent of building a Physically Unclonable Function (PUF). To this end, we specifically analyze the repeatability of data alteration and the information content of the bitflip patterns in modern DDR5 DRAM memories. This analysis would also enable a PUF implementation fully contained within the DRAM module. Our experimental results on multiple DDR5 DRAM chips show that the bitflip patterns caused by read disturbances are remarkably stable when considering a single DRAM row, and are essentially independent across rows in the same bank, or across different devices. These results, combined with a significant information content coming from the unique bitflip pattern produced by a read disturbance, suggest that read-disturbance-based PUFs are promising candidates for future implementations.

title: Hydrogen: Hydrological Report Generation with Two-Stage Instruction-Tuned Multimodal Models, Temporal Prompts, & Knowledge-Guided Agents
authors: Pasith Thanapatpisarn, Kiattikun Chobtham, Kanoksri Sarinnapakorn, Peerapon Vateekul
abstract: Hydrological report generation is crucial for monitoring clouds, typhoons, rainfall, and water events, yet current multimodal models struggle with multi-image alignment and domain-specific knowledge integration. We present HydroGen, a domain-adaptive framework that addresses these challenges through instruction tuning, temporal modeling, and knowledge-guided reasoning within a two-stage pipeline. First, we build HydroMM-Instruct, an instruction dataset that uses YOLOv8 for typhoon detection and shapefiles for region mapping, with reports standardized into cause–effect phrases. Second, we apply a two-stage training pipeline with continual pre-training on hydrological data (radar maps, pressure charts, expert assessments) followed by supervised fine-tuning for report generation. Third, to enhance multi-image alignment, we incorporate temporal prompt tokens that capture event sequences and progressions. Finally, we develop GuideAC, an in-context mechanism that injects antecedent–consequence rules to improve reasoning. Evaluation on Thailand’s weekly hydrological reports (2018–2025) shows that HydroGen substantially outperforms strong multimodal baselines, achieving a BERT-F1 of 84.56% (+46.96%) and a ROUGE-L of 67.78% (+61.48%).

title: Ultra-Flat Gain Profile With Raman Amplification Employing Combined Coherent and Incoherent Pumps_supp1-3696771.pdf
authors: Zoltan Papp
abstract: Distributed Raman amplification is essential for enhancing capacity in wideband optical networks; yet achieving an ultra-flat gain profile remains a significant design challenge. This work presents a validated numerical model incorporating both coherent pump and spectrally broad incoherent pump sources. Model accuracy was rigorously confirmed by demonstrating excellent agreement (average deviation <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$< 0.1\rm { dB}$</tex-math></inline-formula>) between simulation and experiment across various forward, backward, and multi-directional pumping schemes. Leveraging this validated model, we employed an improved simplex optimization algorithm to target ultra-flat amplification across <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$95 \rm { nm}$</tex-math></inline-formula> C+L bands in a <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$40 \rm { km}$</tex-math></inline-formula> fiber. By implementing a hybrid nine-pump scheme (four first-order incoherent and five second-order coherent pumps) to meet practical power limits, we achieved an unprecedented peak-to-peak gain flatness of <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\le 0.04$</tex-math></inline-formula> dB over the 95nm bandwidth, with a <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\leq 0.01\rm { dB}$</tex-math></inline-formula> standard deviation at different target gain values of <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$10\rm { dB}$</tex-math></inline-formula>, <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$9\rm { dB}$</tex-math></inline-formula>, and <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$8\rm { dB}$</tex-math></inline-formula>. Furthermore, we demonstrate the versatility of the system in generating arbitrary gain profiles with high precision. The best performing scheme was thoroughly tested by a Monte Carlo-based stability investigation, resulting in <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\leq 0.04\rm { dB}$</tex-math></inline-formula> standard deviation of gain for practical power and wavelength variations. This result confirms the superior capability of optimally configured hybrid incoherent/coherent pumping for minimizing gain ripple and enabling reconfigurable gain shaping in future high-capacity WDM systems.

title: 3D-Printed Terahertz Porous Fiber for Polarization Control
authors: Liying Lang, Mengqi Gao, Jing Zhang, Yiyang Chen, Mingshuo Fang, Shuai Li, Haisu Li, Yang Cao, Jianqiang Gu
abstract: Polarization-maintaining terahertz fibers are crucial for terahertz wired links and enabling advanced polarization-division multiplexing. While terahertz porous fibers offer great potential for low-loss broadband wave transmission in protected environments, achieving stable polarization control with a reproducible, robust design remains challenging. In this work, we propose a terahertz birefringent porous fiber in a simplified concentric elliptical ring geometry. Through systematic investigation into fiber guiding properties, a porous fiber optimized for operation at 140 GHz is demonstrated. Owing to the enhanced field confinement within its inside air regions, this porous fiber achieves a birefringence on the order of 10⁻² and lower transmission loss compared to its hollow-core counterpart. Such a design is subsequently fabricated using stereolithography 3D printing. We then experimentally characterize the effective refractive index, transmission loss, and near-field imaging of the 3D-printed prototypes, which show good agreement with theoretical predictions. Furthermore, a polarization rotator is implemented by introducing a controlled 90° helical twist along the fiber, achieving a polarization extinction ratio of ∼20 dB across the D-band. We believe that this work provides a practical platform for polarization control in terahertz guided wave, opening new pathways toward integrated terahertz systems for communication and sensing.

title: Physical Origins of the Effect of p-Type Work Function Metal Removal on the Electrical Characteristics in Advanced Gate-All-Around CMOS FET
authors: Kenzo Manabe, Misako Morota, Hiroyuki Ota, Yukinori Morita, Toshifumi Irisawa, Yoshihiro Hayashi
abstract: We investigated the effect of the removal process (wet etching) of p-type work function metal (WFM) (TiN) on electrical characteristics such as effective work function (EWF) and leakage characteristics of high-k (HK) gate dielectric in the nMOS region for CMOS application. We found that the EWF decreased with increasing wet etching amount for TiN removal. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis revealed that the EWF dependence on wet etching amount comes from the Ti residual on the HK, implying that the Ti residual increased the EWF. We proposed that the above EWF increase is attributed to the Ti-residual-induced increase in oxygen content in n-type WFM. We also found that the leakage current increased with increasing wet etching amount for TiN removal. The theoretical calculation of leakage current revealed that the main reason for the increase in leakage current is not the wet damage of the gate dielectric but the EWF decrease induced by the decrease in Ti residual by wet treatment.

title: RIDE: Boosting 3-D Object Detection for LiDAR Point Clouds via Rotation-Invariant Analysis_supp1-3693889.pdf
authors: Xianzhi Li
abstract: The rotation robustness property has drawn much attention to point cloud analysis, whereas it still poses a critical challenge in 3D object detection. When subjected to arbitrary rotation, most existing detectors fail to produce expected outputs due to the poor rotation robustness. In this paper, we present RIDE, a pioneering exploration of Rotation-Invariance for the 3D LiDAR-point-based object DEtector, with the key idea of designing rotation-invariant features from LiDAR scenes and then effectively incorporating them into existing 3D detectors. Specifically, we design a bi-feature extractor that extracts 1) object-aware features that are sensitive to rotation but preserve geometry well, and 2) rotation-invariant features, which lose geometric information to a certain extent but are robust to rotation. These two kinds of features complement each other to decode 3D proposals that are robust to arbitrary rotations. Particularly, our RIDE is compatible and easy to plug into the existing one-stage and two-stage 3D detectors, and boosts both detection performance and rotation robustness. Extensive experiments on the standard benchmarks showcase that the mean average precision (mAP) and rotation robustness can be significantly boosted by integrating with our RIDE, with +5.6% mAP and 111% rotation robustness improvement on KITTI, +5.1% and 34% improvement correspondingly on nuScenes. The code is available at https://github.com/CentauriStar/RIDE

title: Less Signals, More Understanding: Channel-Capacity Codebook Design for Digital Task-Oriented Semantic Communication
authors: Anbang Zhang, Shuaishuai Guo, Chenyuan Feng, Hongyang Du, Haojin Li, Chen Sun, Haijun Zhang
abstract: Discrete representation has emerged as a powerful tool in task-oriented semantic communication (ToSC), offering compact, interpretable, and efficient representations well-suited for low-power edge intelligence scenarios. Its inherent digital nature aligns seamlessly with hardware-friendly deployment and robust storage/transmission protocols. However, current ToSC frameworks often decouple semantic-aware discrete mapping from the underlying channel characteristics and task demands, which leads to suboptimal communication performance and degraded task utility under variable wireless conditions. To address these limitations, this paper proposes a channel-aware discrete semantic coding framework tailored for low-power edge networks. Leveraging a Wasserstein-regularized objective, our approach (WS-VQ) aligns discrete code activations with optimal input distributions, thereby improving semantic fidelity, robustness, and task accuracy. Extensive experiments on the inference tasks across diverse signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) regimes show that our method achieves notable gains in accuracy and communication efficiency. This work provides new insights into integrating discrete semantics and channel optimization, paving the way for the widespread adoption of semantic communication in future digital infrastructures.

title: The adjustment of the turn-to-turn contact resistance of REBCO tape by stripe shape stainless-steel coating
authors: Fulong Sun, Hongli Suo, Zili Zhang, Chengyang Li, Benzhe Zhou, Hongbo Sun, Lei Wang, Jianhua Liu, Qiuliang Wang
abstract: In this paper, the stripe shape stainless-steel coating on the commercial REBCO tape was attempted to adjust the turn-to-turn contact resistance. The original plan is to use a stripe SS coating to create the space between the two REBCO tapes and maintain the path on the SS coating part, which can significantly increase the contact resistance by decreasing the contact area. In real conditions, the SS coating did not reach its goal but created alternative pathways of SS-SS contact and Cu-Cu contact, which can also partially adjust the contact resistance. It is believed that the contact resistance can be controlled in a larger range by changing the coating materials.

title: Learning Invariant Representation for Out-of-distribution Generalization in Photoplethysmography Biometrics
authors: Ran Yi, Yuwen Huang, Gongping Yang, Yilong Yin
publish: IEEE Transactions on Biometrics, Behavior, and Identity Science
abstract: Photoplethysmography (PPG) biometrics have gained significant interest in recent years. However, PPG signals are dynamic and are influenced by physiological and environmental factors, making generalization across their distribution challenging. We propose an invariant representation learning (IRL) framework for PPG biometric recognition to alleviate distribution shift, abbreviated as IRL-PPG. To enhance PPG data diversity, we generate augmented samples via the phase-spectrum perturbation and employ a deep cascade framework comprising a disentanglement 1D vision Transformer (DViT) and a dual feature reconstruction module (DFRM). Within the DViT, a locality-aware feed-forward network and a progressive disentanglement module are employed to separate PPG signals into biometric-invariant and distribution-sensitive features. In the DFRM, to enhance the DViT’s generalization under distribution shifts, we reconstruct the disentangled representations to preserve feature integrity. Specifically, invariant features are utilized for PPG biometrics, while the specific features are isolated to minimize distribution-shift effects on biometric models. Extensive experiments demonstrate that IRL-PPG outperforms state-of-the-art methods across four public PPG databases. The code used in these experiments is available at https://github.com/oniopluto/IRL-PPG.

title: SecureDFL: A Secure Distributed Federated Learning Framework Against Poisoning Attacks
authors: Amir Javadpour, Forough Ja'fari, Tarik Taleb, Fatih Turkmen, Chafika Benzaïd, Mohammad Shojafar
publish: IEEE Transactions on Dependable and Secure Computing
abstract: Distributed Federated Learning (DFL) extends federated learning from a single-server topology to broader graph-based coordination settings that may include multiple interacting aggregation nodes and, in some deployments, externally distributed training resources. While this broader topology improves scalability and flexibility, it also enlarges the poisoning attack surface across participants, aggregators, communication channels, and execution resources. This paper presents SecureDFL, a defense-in-depth framework for poisoning-resilient DFL. SecureDFL integrates authenticated communication, protected aggregation based on additive homomorphic encryption, trust-aware orchestration, multi-point validation, and reinforcement-learning-based mutation of resource mappings under a unified system design. The contribution of this work therefore lies in the integrated security architecture and its end-to-end implementation for graph-based DFL, rather than in introducing a standalone cryptographic primitive. Experiments on benchmark datasets, seven threat-model-aligned poisoning scenarios, ablation studies, and a prototype-scale Kubernetes testbed show that SecureDFL preserves strong model performance under attack and substantially improves attack resilience relative to the evaluated baselines, achieving up to a 98.5% reduction in attack success rate in the reported settings with only modest additional overhead. These results support the practical feasibility of the proposed architecture, while the manuscript explicitly delineates its current limits regarding comprehensive collusion analysis, colluding trust manipulation, extremely heterogeneous non-IID conditions, and fully adaptive multi-stage attacks.

title: Is Mamba Reliable for Medical Imaging?
authors: Banafsheh Saber Latibari, Najmeh Nazari, Daniel Brignac, Hossein Sayadi, Houman Homayoun, Abhijit Mahalanobis
publish: 2026 27th International Symposium on Quality Electronic Design (ISQED)
abstract: State-space models like Mamba offer linear-time sequence processing and low memory, making them attractive for medical imaging. However, their robustness under realistic software and hardware threat models remains underexplored. This paper evaluates Mamba on multiple MedMNIST classification benchmarks under input-level attacks, including white-box adversarial perturbations (FGSM/PGD), occlusion-based PatchDrop, and common acquisition corruptions (Gaussian noise and defocus blur) as well as hardware-inspired fault attacks emulated in software via targeted and random bit-flip injections into weights and activations. We profile vulnerabilities and quantify impacts on accuracy indicating that defenses are needed for deployment.

title: Cognition-Driven Sentiment Analysis: Leveraging Affective Knowledge in Graph Convolutional Networks for Teaching Evaluations_supp1-3690536.pdf
authors: Shoufei Han
abstract: With the proliferation of massive open online courses, teaching evaluation texts have become an important source of feedback, providing valuable affective and cognitive insights into students’ learning experiences. Although automated sentiment analysis techniques have been developed, existing methods still struggle to capture domain-specific affective knowledge and to systematically model the affective and cognitive processes inherent in educational contexts. To address these challenges, we propose an affective cognition-enhanced graph convolutional network (ACEGCN), which simulates a human-like sentiment reasoning process by integrating domain-specific affective cognition knowledge with syntactic information. Specifically, we construct a domain-specific affective cognition knowledge graph by aligning sentiment entities with a general knowledge base. The resulting knowledge is then integrated into the input sentence to form a knowledge-enriched sentence tree, from which semantically enhanced dependency graphs are constructed using a multigranularity syntactic masking mechanism. Finally, we employ a graph convolutional network to capture key sentiment paths and model complex semantic dependencies. Experimental results across three real-world teaching evaluation datasets demonstrate that ACEGCN significantly outperforms baseline models, suggesting its potential to enhance teaching feedback analysis and help improve teaching quality.

title: Modelling of FEOL and BEOL Amorphous Gallium Oxide-based Ferroelectric FETs with a Metal Interlayer for Multilevel Operation in Short Channel Devices
authors: Chiara Rossi, Daniel Lizzit, Charlotte Van Dijck, Thanh Luan Phan, Catherine Dubourdieu, David Esseni
publish: IEEE Journal of the Electron Devices Society
abstract: Ferroelectric field effect transistors (FeFETs) are among the most promising candidates for the implementation of artificial synapses for neuromorphic computing. However, their scaling poses big challenges, as multilevel behavior is hindered by the two-level or very-few-level polarization behavior reported for devices featuring an aggressive lateral scaling. Moreover, the switching and stabilization of ferroelectric polarization, and consequently the device operation, is inherently linked to high levels of charge trapping in the gate stack, limiting the reliability and increasing the variability of the FeFETs. In this work we investigate, by means of calibrated TCAD simulations, FeFETs integrating a metal interlayer inside the gate stack. FeFETs are designed so that, by properly biasing the device, the metal interlayer can get charged thanks to charge tunneling through the dielectric layer. We first investigate a conventional front-end-of-line implementation, consisting of an aggressively scaled FeFET featuring a ferroelectric hafnium zirconium oxide and a dielectric hafnium oxide in the gate stack, a single-crystal silicon channel, and highly-doped source and drain pockets. Then we consider a back-end-of-line compatible device structure featuring lightly– doped amorphous gallium-oxide channel material, with Schottky-type tungsten contacts. We show that, for both architectures, the charge in the metal interlayer can effectively stabilize the ferroelectric polarization, even for a negligible charge trapping in the dielectric stack. Moreover, the interplay between ferroelectric polarization and charge in the metal interlayer enables a multilevel operation even for a uniform ferroelectric polarization.

title: Platform Architecture Approach for Managing Traceability, Visibility, and Risk Management in Freight Transportation
authors: Jonathan Quiroga-Amaya, Erwin J. Sacoto-Cabera, Katiana Tafur-Landazabal, Andrés Castellanos-Ramírez, Jesús Estrada-Gallardo, César Viloria-Núñez
abstract: Freight transportation is one of the largest and fastest-growing industries in the world. The movement of goods is a complex process that involves a wide variety of factors, including environmental factors and transportation routes. This paper proposes a comprehensive platform architecture that integrates real-time tracking, data collection, AI-based analytics, risk management, and liability-based decision making. The proposed platform consists of six interconnected components designed to improve traceability, visibility, and risk management in freight transport. The core components of the proposed platform are real-time tracking, geolocation tracking, user interfaces, liability considerations, and telemetry data collection. The model consists of six components that enable dynamic monitoring and management of freight movement throughout the supply chain. The first component is tracking, and the second focuses on the risk assessment algorithm to evaluate the impact of environmental factors on transportation routes. The third component is liability, providing a robust framework for aligning logistics operations with legal, ethical, and safety standards. Finally, the proposed model offers a vision for a more efficient, secure, and compliant logistics industry. This research lays the foundation for the continued evolution of modern freight transportation systems by integrating advanced technologies and addressing the complexities of the field.

title: Open-Source IEC-Compliant Reconstruction of Turbulent Offshore Wind from Low-Resolution Measurements for Aeroelastic-Relevant Energy Assessment
authors: Luciane A. S. Pedotti, Héctor A. Duarte, Samir C. Gómez, Pedro C. Mello, Renato M. Monaro, Maurício B. C. Salles
abstract: High-frequency turbulent wind data are essential for accurate aeroelastic response analysis, fatigue load estimation, and energy yield assessment in offshore wind turbines. However, offshore measurements are typically available only at coarse temporal resolutions (e.g., hourly), which fail to resolve turbulence structures and introduce systematic bias in capacity factor estimates. This paper presents an open-source, IEC 61400-1-compliant MATLAB framework for reconstructing high-resolution turbulent offshore wind fields from sparse low-resolution time series. The deterministic mean wind component is reconstructed using Piecewise Cubic Hermite Interpolating Polynomial (PCHIP) interpolation, preserving monotonicity and avoiding spurious oscillations. Stochastic turbulent fluctuations are subsequently synthesized using the Kaimal spectral model and the IEC 61400-1 exponential coherence function, generating three-component velocity fields consistent with prescribed turbulence intensity and length scales. The framework is validated against NREL’s TurbSim using statistical (Kolmogorov–Smirnov, Weibull fitting, Shannon entropy), spectral (FFT, wavelet), and temporal (ACF, KPSS) analyses, as well as a comparison of Coherent Turbulent Kinetic Energy (CTKE). Application to an 11-year offshore dataset shows that interpolation alone overestimates the annual capacity factor by 1.35 percentage points, whereas the turbulence-augmented series yields physically consistent estimates with bootstrapped 95% confidence intervals (34.14–43.78%). The proposed framework provides a practical, reproducible, and standards-compliant solution for offshore wind resource assessment and aeroelastic simulations when high-resolution measurements are unavailable.

title: Efficiently Combining Iterated Local Search and Decomposition: A Comprehensive Study on Multi-Objective Binary Landscapes
authors: Bilel Derbel
abstract: We propose a hybrid framework for binary multi-objective combinatorial optimization that integrates iterated local search with decomposition. The key idea is to perform local search in a cooperative manner when solving the aggregated subproblems induced by decomposition, while using perturbation to escape local optima.We systematically investigate different design options in which local search is employed either as a standalone component or as a tightly integrated cooperative search engine. In particular, we introduce a method that embeds local search within decomposition in a fine-grained manner, enabling cooperative neighborhood exploration to accelerate convergence toward high-quality solutions. In this framework, perturbation acts as a soft-restart mechanism when locally optimal solutions are reached for individual sub-problems. We conduct an extensive experimental study on a broad range of binary landscapes to assess the effectiveness of the proposed hybridization in comparison with recently proposed techniques from the literature. Our empirical results demonstrate that the proposed approach consistently achieves superior results and exhibits a strong anytime efficiency. In addition, we analyze the impact of problem structure and key algorithmic components, such as perturbation strength and the choice of scalarization function, providing insights into the behavior and dynamics of the proposed hybrid approach.

title: Enhanced High-Temperature Reliability of Wire-Bonded 3D SiC Chip Packaging Using Patterned Ti/TaSi₂/Pt Metallization and SiO₂ Insulation
authors: Jiaqi Shi, Buddhi S. Lamsal, Feng Li
publish: IEEE Transactions on Components, Packaging and Manufacturing Technology
abstract: This paper presents an improved wire-bonding-based three-dimensional (3D) silicon carbide (SiC) integrated circuit (IC) packaging structure designed for high-temperature operation up to 600 °C. The work targets packaging of SiC logic and memory chips, addressing the need for reliable system-level integration in extreme environments such as aerospace propulsion control and planetary surface exploration. A tantalum silicide (TaSi₂) layer was introduced between titanium (Ti) and platinum (Pt) layers to act as a diffusion barrier against oxygen ingress and gold interdiffusion. In addition, a silicon dioxide (SiO₂) insulation layer was incorporated to suppress leakage currents. Furthermore, patterned metallization was implemented to replicate the pad geometry of practical SiC IC devices. During the 600 °C thermal aging test, daisy chain resistance decreased from approximately 4.7 Ω to 3.2 Ω and stabilized. The wire pull strength stabilized at about 3.7 gram-force (gf) after 2 days of thermal exposure. The die shear strength for chip-to-substrate attachment increased from 11.5 kilogram-force (kgf) to approximately 14 kgf. The die shear strength for chip-to-chip attachment remained stable at around 10.5 kgf. All measured pull and shear strengths satisfied MIL-STD-883 Methods 2011.9 and 2019.9, confirming high interconnect integrity. These results demonstrate the feasibility of a 3D wire-bonded SiC IC packaging platform with Ti/TaSi₂/Pt metallization and SiO₂ insulation, enabling long-duration, high-temperature operation of SiC logic and memory systems in harsh environments.

title: Ultra-thin broadband optical skin based on graphene-stack electrochemical structure via ion intercalation tuning
authors: Xing Cao, Kaixi Bi, Longhao Liu, Guangchen Yin, Jinxian Dong, Lei Hu, Yichi Zhang, Linyu Mei, Xiujian Chou
abstract: Smart optical skins play a critical role in camouflage and intelligent thermal management. Nevertheless, realizing broadband optical modulation within ultra-thin display architectures continue to pose a substantial technical challenge. Graphene’s zero-bandgap structure facilitates broadband optical absorption modulation through the continuous modulation of its Fermi level. Among various methods, electrochemical intercalation stands out for its low power consumption and robust cycling stability based on an ultra-thin film architecture. This study introduces an innovative optical display panel based on the lithium-ion electrochemical intercalation method. Experimental results demonstrate that dynamic modulation of the Fermi level achieves a reflectance modulation of 43.07% across the visible spectrum and a transmittance modulation of 35.75% within the long-wave infrared region. A 64-pixel array device—fabricated using a fully flexible material system—achieves a total integration thickness of only 225 μm. The device exhibits a rapid spectral switching time of 491 ms, while maintaining pixel dimensions below 3 mm × 3 mm. Moreover, the modulated graphene sustains a temperature differential of 17.2 °C with respect to the background temperature of the heat source. This study establishes both a robust physical foundation and a scalable technical framework to advance the development of multispectral surfaces and next-generation wearable electronics.

title: An Efficient Approach with Theoretical Guarantees to Simultaneously Reconstruct Activity and Attenuation Sinogram for TOF-PET_supp1-3697651.pdf
authors: Chong Chen
abstract: In positron emission tomography (PET), it is indispensable to perform attenuation correction in order to obtain quantitatively accurate activity (tracer distribution) in the body. Generally, this is carried out based on the regulated or synthetic attenuation map obtained by computed tomography or magnetic resonance imaging respectively. However, except for errors in the attenuation correction factors obtained, the additional scan not only brings in more radiation dose and/or increases the scanning time but also leads to severe misalignments induced by the body motion during and between the two sequential scans. To address these issues, based on Maximum Likelihood estimation, we propose a new mathematical model for simultaneously reconstructing the Activity and Attenuation Sinogram, referred to as MLAAS, from time-of-flight (TOF)-PET emission data only. Particularly, in the proposed model, we make full use of the exclusively exponential form of the attenuation correction factors, and consider the constraint on a total amount of the activity in some mask region. Furthermore, we analyze the well-posedness of the model, including the existence, uniqueness and stability of its solution. We propose an alternating update algorithm to solve the model, and also analyze its convergence. Finally, numerical experiments with various emission data demonstrate that the proposed method MLAAS is of numerical convergence and robust to noise, and outperforms some state-of-the-art methods in terms of accuracy and efficiency, and enables TOF-PET to have the capability of autonomous attenuation correction.

title: A*VL: Incorporating A* and Visual Perception for High-Precision Vehicle Localization
authors: Huei-Yung Lin, Yu-Xuan Chen, Hao-Che Hsu
abstract: With continuous advances of autonomous driving technologies, the demand for precise vehicle positioning has grown significantly. Standard civilian GPS still suffers from positioning errors of approximately 3 to 5 meters, which is insufficient for rapidly evolving advanced driver assistance systems requiring the lane-level accuracy. In this paper, we present A*VL, a high-precision vehicle localization framework based on driving images and satellite maps. Instead of relying on the expensive LiDAR systems or high-definition maps, the proposed method extracts environmental features from driving scenes through lane detection, semantic segmentation, and geolocation, while satellite image retrieve from coarse GPS coordinates. More importantly, we incorporate the A*-based path-aware loss into training process, which transforms road boundary constraints into learnable penalty signals and also encourages the predicted localization results to remain consistent with road topology rather than merely fitting coordinate values. In addition, several datasets containing satellite maps and latitude/longitude annotations of diverse road scenes are established for evaluation and benchmarking. Experimental results and ablation analyses demonstrate that the proposed A* constraint effectively improves vehicle positioning performance. Several datasets containing satellite maps and latitude/longitude coordinates of various road scenes are established for evaluation and benchmarking. Code and datasets are available publicly at https://github.com/JimHsu0007/vehicle-positioning.

title: Inhomogeneous Plasma Parameters Inversion Based on Convolutional Neural Network
authors: Liping Gan, Lixin Guo, Haichao Zhan, Hao Du
abstract: Electron density, collision frequency, and plasma thickness are important parameters for characterizing plasma properties. Thus, obtaining these parameters accurately is a prerequisite for studying the interaction between plasma and electromagnetic (EM) waves. In this study, the electron density, collision frequency, and thickness of inhomogeneous plasma were estimated through a convolutional neural network (CNN). Compared with traditional plasma diagnostic methods, the CNN avoids complicated calculations and experimental construction. The performance of the proposed approach was evaluated and analyzed by comparison with the backpropagation (BP) neural network. Results indicate that the CNN can be used to estimate plasma parameters based on the reflection and transmission waves. Furthermore, the CNN provides better performance than the BP neural network to retrieve plasma electron density distribution, and the electron density distribution mean absolute percentage errors (MAPEs) are less than 2%. The greater the range variation of electron density, the greater the relative errors will be. Importantly, through the CNN, multiple plasma parameters can be inverted simultaneously. For collision frequency and plasma thickness, the outputs of the CNN are also consistent with the test data. This study provides an important theoretical reference for diagnosing inhomogeneous plasma parameters.

title: Two-Stage Nested Magnetic Field Control System Based on Time-Frequency Domain Collaboration for Magnetically Shielded Room
authors: Yimin Chen, Suhang Li, Bangcheng Han, Xiaoting Hong, Haifeng Zhang, Fengwen Zhao, Rui Feng
abstract: The complex electromagnetic disturbances often result in excessively high magnetic field noise. This article proposes a two-stage nested magnetic field control system based on time-frequency domain collaboration for a magnetically shielded room (MSR) to suppress the disturbance magnetic field and reduce magnetic field noise. The system creates a high-bandwidth external control system by placing external auxiliary coils and a fluxgate sensor outside the MSR, avoiding mutual interference with the internal control system. Subsequently, the system uses a self-tuning proportional-integral-derivative (PID) based on pole placement control and the gradient algorithm based on discrete Fourier transform to obtain the optimal internal noise level. This proposed method has effectively increased the control bandwidth while avoiding the design of complex two-stage decoupling strategies. Compared with the traditional only internal control system, the average noise suppression ability within the target frequency band has been improved by more than 20<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\mathbf{\%}$</tex-math> </inline-formula>.

title: Breaking the Communication-Accuracy Trade-off: A Sparsified Information Diffusion Framework for Multi-Agent Collaborative Perception_supp1-3689715.pdf
authors: Xinlei Chen
abstract: The growing relevance of multi-agent systems has drawn increasing focus on communication-efficient filters for collaborative perception to alleviate the system's communication burden. While the event-triggered (ET) mechanism can improve communication efficiency in collaborative state estimation, an inevitable trade-off exists between estimation accuracy and communication cost in ET filters. This paper proposes a fast and accurate ET diffusion-based filter for real-time multi-agent collaborative target tracking, aiming to reduce the system's data transmission without compromise in tracking performance. The proposed filter achieves improved tracking accuracy, reduced data transmission, and accelerated convergence using an error-minimized ET cubature information filter (CIF) for local estimation, and a correlation-aware diffusion strategy for global fusion. The experimental results confirm the scalability of the proposed EDC-CIF algorithm and demonstrate its efficacy in simultaneously reducing estimation error and computation time while significantly enhancing communication efficiency.

title: Future-Trend-Aware Filter-Based PD-MRAC Method for Quadrotors With Unknown Strong Disturbances_supp1-3696040.mp4
authors: Yanhua Yang
abstract: Robust flight in complex and windy environments is critical for both single and multiple quadrotors. Existing methods either learn disturbance model at high computational cost or use error-based adaptive control with a speed-stability trade-off that makes tuning difficult. To address these issues, this paper proposes a future-trend-aware filter-based PD-MRAC (Proportional-Derivative Model Reference Adaptive Control) for single quadrotor and a distributed PD-MRAC for multiple quadrotor formation. By embedding a trend-aware derivative term in the adaptive update laws, the controller obtains anticipatory information about the error evolution, enabling rapid adaptation while mitigating oscillations. For more disturbance-sensitive multi-quadrotors, we design a robust distributed protocol under a directed graph, improving resilience to disturbances. The approach maintains low computational cost and supports fast adaptive updates. Extensive simulations and real-world experiments validate improvement. For single quadrotor, RMSE reduced by around 57% versus the baselines and by around 12% versus the DJI Mavic 2. For multi-quadrotors, formation results show enhanced robustness in simulation and effective real-world indoor/outdoor experiments under strong winds. Our project page is at <uri xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">https://xiongtao-shi.github.io/PD-MRAC/</uri>.

title: Radar Measurement Perception against Dense Deceptive Jamming: A Unified Transformer for Classification, Association, and Tracking
authors: Wenbin Wei, Rui Guo, Zengping Chen
abstract: Dense deceptive jamming generates voluminous false measurements and severely degrades radar association and tracking performance. To address this problem, we propose a unified radar point-level perception architecture based on an encoder-only Transformer. Operating in a streaming slidingwindow manner, the proposed framework jointly performs authenticity classification, association-oriented representation learning, and geometry-aware measurement refinement. By exploiting long-term temporal cues, including macroscopic kinematic dependence and mechanism-induced intra-cluster structural regularities of dense false plots, the framework improves real-false discrimination under severe measurement contamination. To alleviate feature homogeneity between real and false measurements, we introduce a clutter-aware supervised contrastive learning strategy with virtual negative classes, which enhances the discriminability of the learned association embeddings. We further develop an association-guided geometric refinement module that aggregates historical context for trajectory smoothing, together with a radial-tangential decoupled loss to better accommodate anisotropic radar measurement errors. In addition, a confidenceguided soft gating mechanism with gradient isolation is incorporated to reduce negative transfer from low-confidence false plots during multi-task learning. Experiments on the evaluated dense deceptive jamming simulation datasets show that the proposed method improves both association accuracy and tracking precision over the compared approaches.

title: Quantifying Predictive Uncertainty in Hybrid Deep Learning Architectures for Multi-Sensor Forecasting of Nuclear Reactor Cooling Systems
authors: Ibnu Hajar, Murizah Kassim, Mohd Sabri Minhat, Syamsir Abduh
abstract: Reliable forecasting of nuclear reactor cooling system parameters is essential for safety and early anomaly detection in safety-critical environments. While deep learning models perform well on supervisory control and data acquisition (SCADA) time-series data, most remain deterministic and lack rigorous uncertainty assessment under realistic conditions. This article proposes an uncertainty-aware forecasting framework using hybrid deep learning architectures integrated with Monte Carlo (MC) dropout for multisensor nuclear reactor cooling systems. Three hybrid models—gated recurrent unit (GRU)–WaveNet, GRU–long short-term memory (LSTM), and GRU–LSTM–WaveNet—are evaluated using real SCADA data from a research reactor secondary cooling loop, covering temperature, pressure, and flow rate sensors. In single-step-ahead forecasting, all models achieve high accuracy, with RMSE values as low as 0.717 for temperature sensors and R2 values up to 0.916 for pressure signals. However, walk-forward validation reveals significant error accumulation, with RMSE increasing to 46.53 for PT003 and R2 values dropping to near zero or negative for PT002 and FT002, highlighting limitations of conventional offline evaluation. Predictive uncertainty is assessed using prediction interval coverage probability (PICP), prediction interval normalized average width, calibration error, and coverage width-based criterion. Empirical PICP values range from 72.5% for temperature sensors to as low as 31.5% for flow signals at a nominal 95% confidence level, indicating systematic undercoverage. Among the evaluated architectures, the GRU–WaveNet model provides the most balanced tradeoff between accuracy, uncertainty sharpness, calibration error, and computational efficiency, with an inference latency of 38.1 ms/sample, compared with 51.5 ms/sample for deeper hybrids. These results demonstrate that uncertainty-aware forecasting for nuclear SCADA systems must jointly consider accuracy, calibration, and deployability–point prediction performance alone.

title: HeXinTimer: An AI-Enhanced STA Framework Supporting CCS and SI with Signoff Accuracy
authors: Chao Yan, Zishu Wu, Jingjia Xu, Ziwei Liu, Boyuan Liu, Xi Chen, Zefeng Wang
publish: 2026 27th International Symposium on Quality Electronic Design (ISQED)
abstract: STA tools play a critical role in signoff, requiring exceptional accuracy to ensure circuit reliability. HeXinTimer is an industry-grade STA solution designed to support advanced modeling with high precision. This work introduces the following key contributions: (1) a sophisticated stage delay calculation methodology that incorporates Composite Current Source (CCS) driver and receiver models with distributed RC networks, achieving superior timing accuracy; (2) a hybrid framework that combines traditional SI flow with AI predictors, utilizing internal tool data for both training and inference, which significantly enhances accuracy and performance in delta delay estimation; (3) a robust and efficient root-finding algorithm that integrates the Traub-Ostrowski iteration and bisection method to enhance computational performance; and (4) a stage-based testing and comparison framework for comprehensive validation. HeXinTimer achieves accuracy within $3\%$ of SPICE results, while maintaining performance comparable to industry-leading tools.

title: BM-HPR Mul: Error Tolerant Hardware Efficient Booth Modified High Precision Redundant Multiplier
authors: Chandan Kumar N S, Yerasi Manoj Reddy, Madhav Rao
publish: 2026 27th International Symposium on Quality Electronic Design (ISQED)
abstract: High-precision and fault-tolerant multipliers are indispensable in energy-constrained computing platforms that operate under transient and process-induced errors. Conventional Triple Modular Redundancy (TMR) ensures strong reliability but suffers from high area and power overheads. The High-Precision Redundancy Multiplier (HPR-Mul) mitigates these costs through selective redundancy however, its tunable parameter introduces synthesis irregularities and inconsistent fault resilience. This work presents the Booth-Modified High-Precision Redundancy Multiplier (BM-HPR), which integrates Radix-4 Booth encoding into both full-precision (FP) and reduced-precision (RP) paths while eliminating the tunable parameter to achieve a fixed, synthesis-friendly datapath. The proposed design preserves the compact area of HPR while achieving up to 27.7% area reduction, 65.1% lower power consumption, and 31.8% faster delay compared to TMR. Additionally, it improves delay and power by 19.1% and 53.7%, respectively, over HPR. Fault-injection using random bit-flip models confirms bounded error rates suitable for error-resilient applications. When deployed in a $\mu$-law companding audio system, BM-HPR maintains a stable signal-tonoise ratio (SNR) and low error variance under injected faults, demonstrating its practical applicability. Implemented using the OpenROAD 45 nm flow, BM-HPR demonstrates a reproducible, energy-efficient, and fault-tolerant multiplier architecture suitable for embedded and real-time signal-processing workloads. All design files are made publicly available for reproducibility.

title: Robustness Analysis of Neuromorphic Embodied AI Robot for Associative Learning against Adversarial Stickers
authors: Tianze Liu, Kang Jun Bai, Hongyu An
publish: 2026 27th International Symposium on Quality Electronic Design (ISQED)
abstract: Neuromorphic embodied Artificial Intelligence (AI) robots have shown the capability to perform associative learning, emulating how animals learn through interactions with their environments. Through such process, the neuromorphic embodied AI robot can memorize concurrent stimuli, such as vibrations and visual cues. However, these sensory inputs often contain perturbations, such as visual adversarial stickers, that can distort perception and interfere with learned associations. This study presents a robust vision processing framework inspired by the animal visual cortex and system, e.g., V1, for a neuromorphic robot capable of detecting and suppressing the interference from adversarial stickers. The proposed system integrates this visual processing module with a neuromorphic embodied AI robot, along with an associative learning mechanism. The robot is evaluated in an open-field maze, where it learns to associate neutral visual landmarks with vibration as an aversive stimulus. Both simulation and experimental results demonstrate that the proposed approach maintains high navigation accuracy and stable spatial memory even in the presence of adversarial stickers. Analyzes of trajectories and neural firing patterns further demonstrate that the neuromorphic embodied AI robot with enhanced visual system effectively resists deceptive input and preserves accurate associative learning.

title: DUA: Detection of Unrecognized Applications Using One-Class SVM in NoC-Based SoCs
authors: Andrea Galimberti, Katsuaki Nakano, Rohan Purkait, Amlan Ganguly, Michael Zuzak, Mark A. Indovina, Sai Manoj Pudukotai Dinakarrao, William Fornaciari, Davide Zoni
publish: 2026 27th International Symposium on Quality Electronic Design (ISQED)
abstract: Unrecognized applications running on multi-core SoCs may signal system compromise and increase vulnerability. This paper introduces DUA, a novel methodology to detect the execution of unrecognized software applications on NoC-based multi-core SoCs, leveraging traffic data from the NoC interconnect, or, more broadly, any packet-switched on-chip or interposerlevel interconnect such as a network-in-package. DUA embeds lightweight traffic-monitoring counters into selected routers of the interconnect, and it carries out detection using a one-class support vector machine (OC-SVM) that, due to its unsupervisedlearning nature, is trained only on recognized applications, i.e., applications authorized to run on the system. This allows the DUA methodology to effectively detect unrecognized, never-before-seen applications, including those not considered during training. Identifying the optimal OC-SVM involves exploring a search space that encompasses input features, kernel functions, and hyperparameters. An extensive experimental campaign demonstrates the effectiveness of the methodology in detecting unrecognized applications from the PARSEC benchmark suite on a 16 -core NoC-based SoC. The DUA approach achieves an average accuracy of 85.9% while introducing negligible area and power overheads and not affecting timing, and it shows resilience to queuing-delay noise, indicating generalization across varying traffic conditions.

title: MSFFC: A novel dimensionality reduction method for improving maize nitrogen estimation from high-dimensional spectral reflectance data
authors: Hang Yin, Haibo Yang, Fei Li, Yuncai Hu, Kang Yu
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: Due to strong inter-band correlations and redundancy, accurately extracting information related to crop canopy nitrogen content (CNC) from hyperspectral data remains challenging. To address this issue, this study proposes a multi-strategy feature fusion and compression (MSFFC) method that integrates optimal band subsets selected by different feature selection methods and further compresses redundant information using an autoencoder (AE). This design improves the retention of complementary spectral information while alleviating the influence of feature correlation on CNC inversion. Based on UAV hyperspectral images and CNC data from maize under different nitrogen fertilizer gradients during 2020-2022, fifteen feature selection methods were evaluated. Subsequently, AE was employed to compress the optimal single feature subset and the fused feature subset to reduce redundancy. Five commonly used machine learning models and the Tabular Prior-data Fitted Network (TabPFN) were employed as evaluation functions and modeling tools for the dimensionality reduction method. Although the successive projection algorithm (SPA) demonstrated the best performance among all feature selection methods, integrating SPA with AE did not further improve model accuracy. In contrast, MSFFC achieved a significant reduction in model RMSE, ranging from 0.9% to 38.7% while reducing full-band hyperspectral data by 97.6%. The robustness of models constructed using SPA and MSFFC features was compared on the external dataset, the PROSAIL-PRO simulation dataset, and the public dataset. The results showed that the quality of the input features has a greater impact on model performance than the dataset's size. MSFFC mitigates the estimation limitations of traditional artificial neural network models in small-sample scenarios (< 100 samples). Among all models, MSFFC-TabPFN demonstrated the best performance, achieving a 25.9%-123.2% improvement in the composite performance index (CPI). These results highlight the critical role of combining feature selection methods with nonlinear feature compression in hyperspectral research, providing essential methodological support for robust, generalizable crop parameter inversion models.

title: Impacts of Marketing Strategies on Supply Chain Resilience and Sustainability
authors: Towfique Rahman, Sanjoy Kumar Paul, Tapan Sarker, Nagesh Shukla, Harsha Sarvaiya
abstract: In today's volatile world, organizations need to think about how marketing strategies can be aligned with supply chain (SC) resilience to drive business performance. This study, therefore, aims to investigate the integration of marketing-led demand creation and adaptive and resilient SC strategies and how they can improve agility, profitability and sustainability outcomes. Marketing strategies of the organizations aim to accelerate growth through promotions and engagement with customers. However, it often creates operational stress on SCs to scale up production rapidly. Centralized SC models, which are efficient in stable conditions, are often vulnerable to disruptions when demand surges. This situation results in inefficiencies and reputational risks for the organizations. Adopting a simulation-optimization based methodology, this research models SC configurations under dynamic demand scenarios, which are influenced by strategies led by marketing strategies. The study's key findings show that decentralization, flexibility and proactive SC design improve service reliability and customer demand satisfaction. Along with this, resilient SCs contribute to sustainability by reducing emissions to the environment and improving resource utilization. The study further emphasizes the need for a combined marketing, operations and sustainability strategic approach to co-develop integrated solutions. This paper contributes to knowledge management by bridging gaps between demand generation, operational readiness and sustainability. It proposes a novel methodological framework for designing sustainable SCs that are agile, customer-focused and environmentally friendly and thus offer actionable insights for scholars, practitioners and policymakers.

title: Towards SDG 7: A Quantitative Sustainability Framework for Evaluating PV+BES Systems in the Low-Carbon Transition
authors: Idiano D'Adamo, Simone Di Leo, Massimo Gastaldi, Matteo Scarcelli
abstract: Sustainability in the energy sector requires a pragmatic and interdisciplinary approach. This study analyses the economic profitability and environmental effectiveness of photovoltaic (PV) systems integrated with battery energy storage (BES) systems through a quantitative framework based on Net Present Value analysis, sensitivity analysis, Least Absolute Shrinkage and Selection Operator regression and greenhouse gas emission reduction value, using a shopping mall in Rome as a case study. By applying uniform percentage changes to economic and environmental variables, the study identifies the critical factors that influence profitability in different self-consumption scenarios. The results show that, in PV systems, the avoided cost in the electricity bill (energy purchase price) is the most significant lever, especially in contexts with high self-consumption, while the sale price becomes more important when self-consumption is low. In BES systems, increased self-consumption remains the main factor improving profitability, although investment costs continue to have a significant impact. Tax deductions have less impact, especially in mature markets. From an environmental point of view, the reduction in emissions depends heavily on the composition of the fossil fuel mix replaced, with a limited additional environmental impact from BES. The results offer operational guidance for prosumers, energy managers and investors to achieve SDG 7 by optimising self-consumption rate, electricity purchase price, PV+BES investment cost, and the fossil-fuel mix replaced to improve the economic and environmental performance of PV+BES systems.
