title: Thickness Dependence of Anisotropic Magnetoresistance in CoPt Alloy Multilayer Nanowires Fabricated by Dual-Bath Electrodeposition
authors: Rio Kawana, Daiki Oshima, Mikiko Saito, Takayuki Homma, Takeshi Kato, Teruo Ono, Mutsuhiro Shima, Keisuke Yamada
abstract: We investigated the dependence of anisotropic magnetoresistance (AMR) on the layer thickness and Co composition of Co-rich/Pt-rich CoPt alloy multilayer nanowires fabricated by dual-bath electrodeposition using nanoporous templates. Single-nanowire current-perpendicular-to-plane (CPP) electrical transport measurements revealed that reducing the individual layer thickness to a few nanometers significantly enhanced the AMR ratio, yielding a value that was 2.7 times higher than that of single-layer CoPt alloy nanowires. This enhancement might be attributed to increased interfacial spin-dependent scattering in the multilayer structure under CPP transport. In addition, the Co composition of the Co-rich layers had little effect on the AMR ratio in the high-field saturation region. In contrast, clear differences in AMR behavior were observed at low magnetic fields, indicating that the Co composition influenced the magnetization rotation process rather than the intrinsic anisotropic resistance. These findings elucidated the mechanisms underlying AMR enhancement in multilayer nanowires and provided insight into interface-dominated spin-dependent transport for the development of high-density magnetic sensors and magnetic memory devices.

title: Automatic Identification of Jiles–Atherton Hysteresis Model Parameters from B–H Loops
authors: Jônatas Policarpo Américo, Jean Vianei Leite, Kleiton De Morais Sousa, Cesar Rafael Claure Torrico, Nelson Jhoe Batistela
abstract: This paper presents JA-AutoFit, a structured framework for the automated identification of Jiles–Atherton (JA) scalar magnetic hysteresis model parameters from B–H loops. The methodology integrates analytical relations derived from the JA equations with the automated extraction of characteristic loop features, such as local differential susceptibilities at key operating points. A coupled iterative scheme is employed to resolve the five macroscopic parameters that govern the magnetic behavior in the JA model, while a two-stage grid search combined with time-domain simulations ensures global consistency by minimizing the mean squared reconstruction error. The approach is validated using experimental measurements under low, intermediate, and nominal induction regimes, obtained from distinct sources including a Single Sheet Tester on transformer core laminations. Additionally, the algorithm's robustness is evaluated using a benchmark of 248 synthetic loops encompassing diverse materials, alongside specific stress-tests under deep saturation and systematic induction offsets. Performance is compared against genetic algorithm, particle swarm optimization, pattern search, simulated annealing, and multi-start Nelder–Mead methods. Statistical analysis via Friedman test and Nemenyi post-hoc comparisons (p < 10–6) demonstrates that JA-AutoFit achieves superior accuracy and robustness across all evaluated scenarios, effectively eliminating the need for manual initialization or expert-defined search spaces.

title: Human-in-the-Loop-Based Secure Prescribed-Time Cooperative Path Following Control for Uncrewed Surface Vehicles
authors: Jawhar Ghommam, Maarouf Saad, Juan Wang, Quanmin Zhu
abstract: This article presents a human-in-the-loop cooperative control framework for uncrewed surface vessels that executes high-level human commands specifying formation scale and collective speed within a preassignable time. A novel control framework is proposed, integrating a secure encryption-decryption protocol for intervehicle communication with a dynamic event-triggering mechanism to minimize network usage. To address the nonsmooth control signals arising from this setup, a prescribed-time command filter is introduced to avoid explicit differentiation of virtual control signals and to generate smooth implementable reference derivatives. The framework also includes an event-triggered controller to reduce onboard computational resources. Theoretical analysis guarantees boundedness of all closed-loop signals and prescribed-time convergence of the tracking errors under the imposed disturbance and uncertainty conditions, while excluding Zeno behavior. Simulation results validate the effectiveness and performance of the proposed scheme.

title: Structure-Aware Local Differential Privacy for Multimodal Collaborative Inference in Federated Learning Systems
authors: Yuan Gao, Yingjie Xia, Xuejiao Liu, Oubo Ma, Shuaifan Jin, Jia-Nan Liu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Dependable and Secure Computing
abstract: Cloud-edge collaborative inference (CI) in federated learning systems for multimodal tasks exposes intermediate features to potential reconstruction attacks. Local differential privacy (LDP), as a widely used privacy-preserving paradigm, has emerged as a practical defense for collaborative inference. Nevertheless, most existing LDP-based methods inject uniform noise across modalities, without accounting for differences in task importance and privacy sensitivity, leading to a suboptimal trade off where task-critical dimensions may be over-perturbed while sensitive components remain inadequately protected. To address these issues, we propose Struct-DP, a structure-aware framework featuring two core mechanisms: (i) shared-private representation decoupling, which utilizes cross-modal correlation learning to iso late task-relevant shared semantics; and (ii) structure-aware noise injection, which adaptively distributes the noise budget based on dimension-wise privacy sensitivity and task importance scores. By allocating more noise to high-privacy, low-task subspaces, our scheme achieves a better balance between privacy protection and task utility. Extensive evaluations on two prevailing datasets (CMU-MOSI and CMU-MOSEI) show that Struct-DP retains 98.2% of the peak task utility while increasing the reconstruction error (MSE) by approximately 7× over uniform-noise baselines.

title: MOTION: ML-Assisted On-Device Low-Latency Motion Recognition
authors: Veeramani Pugazhenthi, Wei-Hsiang Chu, Junwei Lu, Jadyn N. Miyahira, Mahdi Eslamimehr, Pratik Satam, Rozhin Yasaei, Soheil Salehi
publish: 2026 27th International Symposium on Quality Electronic Design (ISQED)
abstract: The use of tiny devices capable of low-latency gesture recognition is gaining momentum in everyday human-computer interaction and especially in medical monitoring fields. Embedded solutions such as fall detection, rehabilitation tracking, and patient supervision require fast and efficient tracking of movements while avoiding unwanted false alarms. This study presents an efficient solution on how to build very efficient motion-based models only using triaxial accelerometer sensors. We explore the capability of the AutoML pipelines to extract the most important features from the data segments. This approach also involves training multiple lightweight machine learning algorithms using the extracted features. We use WeBe Band, a multi-sensor wearable device that is equipped with a powerful enough MCU to effectively perform gesture recognition entirely on the device. Of the models explored, we found that the neural network provided the best balance between accuracy, latency, and memory use. Our results also demonstrate that reliable real-time gesture recognition can be achieved in WeBe Band, with great potential for real-time medical monitoring solutions that require a secure and fast response time.

title: Power-Off Laser Attack Against Bulk Built-in Current Sensors
authors: Raphael Viera, Noé Backert, Jean-Max Dutertre
publish: 2026 27th International Symposium on Quality Electronic Design (ISQED)
abstract: This paper investigates a novel attack vector targeting security primitives through laser illumination applied during circuit power cycles or power-off states, scenarios relevant to supply chain threats where adversaries may access devices before deployment. Specifically, we examine the vulnerabilities of the Bulk Built-In Current Sensor, a countermeasure designed to detect laser fault injection by monitoring laser-induced currents in the silicon bulk of integrated circuits. Our study evaluates whether laser illumination applied while the circuit is powered off can be used to disable or permanently bias these sensors, enabling subsequent undetected attacks during normal operation.

title: A System-Level Design-Space Exploration of Security for Systems-on-Chip
authors: Abigail Butka, Antonio Hendricks, Christophe Bobda
publish: 2026 27th International Symposium on Quality Electronic Design (ISQED)
abstract: This paper introduces a novel design automation approach for securing Systems-on-Chip (SoCs) by natively integrating security as a primary optimization objective. Unlike existing design automation solutions that typically explore implementations for specific vulnerabilities, our method utilizes a purpose-built security metric specifically tailored for SoCs. Our optimization algorithm accepts a system specification (either as an application or an abstract SoC description) and generates a secured SoC architecture. This architecture enhances the regular SoC with an optimized security component. Optimization goals include critical metrics such as security, area, power, and latency, which are quantified as system risk. As a proof of concept, the proposed DSE of security tested three distinct security optimization strategies on a single system architecture: unrestricted resource and power utilization, restricted resource utilization, and restricted power utilization. The optimizer effectively returns all valid solutions that adhere to the user’s defined risk constraints, clearly showcasing the inherent trade-offs between optimized system risk, Look-Up Table (LUT) utilization, and power consumption. The results demonstrate how designers can leverage this tool to identify optimal security and anomaly detection solutions tailored to their specific system architectures. For instance, while the most secure solution might demand thousands of LUTs, our tool facilitates balanced decision-making by considering security alongside resource, power, and latency constraints. This allows designers to incorporate security directly into the design process.

title: MiniBGTR: Mini-Binary Graph Transformer for Facial Expression Recognition
authors: Xing Jin, Xulin Song, Xiyin Wu, Qiaolin Ye
publish: IEEE Transactions on Computational Social Systems
abstract: Deep graph convolution networks (GCNs) can be exploited for facial expression recognition (FER) by modeling irregular muscle movements. However, existing graph convolution operation suffers from a limited receptive field due to the first-order neighbor nodes model’s ability. The existing solution is stacking multiple graph convolution layers, leading to performance degradation due to the over-smoothing phenomenon. Moreover, the deeper graph network limits the efficiency and scalability of applications on edge devices. To alleviate these issues, we develop a simple yet effective mini-binary graph-transformer hybrid architecture, in which the transformer strengthens the global modeling ability of different facial muscles, and the binary operation enables real-time detection by significantly reducing computational complexity. Specifically, facial action units (AUs) associated regions are used to construct image tokens. By combining the complementary graph convolution and transformer, we propose a lightweight and efficient mini-graph transformer (MiniGTR) to interact on complex relations among facial AUs. Finally, we propose a novel binary architecture termed MiniBGTR which is designed to leverage the binarization operation in the MiniGTR. From the perspective of accuracy, parameters and model size, we evaluate their effectiveness on four facial expression datasets. Furthermore, binary design led to lower computational complexity while maintaining more promising results compared to other deep networks.

title: BATTER-MoE: A Sparse Mixture-of-Experts Model for Accurate and Efficient Remaining Useful Life Prediction of Lithium-Ion Batteries
authors: Xuan Yi, Jianmao Xiao, Gang Lei, Xin Hu, Musheng Wu, Bo Xu, Chuying Ouyang
abstract: Accurately estimating the remaining useful life (RUL) of batteries from degradation trajectories is critical for system safety but remains challenging due to the complex and heterogeneous nature of degradation processes. Existing dense predictors achieve strong accuracy but process heterogeneous degradation trajectories through a shared full-capacity pathway, which may limit deployment flexibility in resource-constrained settings due to their computational cost. To address this limitation, we propose BATTER-MoE, a sparse Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) framework that couples battery-oriented temporal representation learning with sparse conditional computation. The framework combines time-aware multi-scale tokenization and cross-scale feature fusion with temporal reweighting, a sparse MoE backbone with RoPE-driven self-attention and a shared expert mechanism, and time-aware pooling for prediction. Experimental results on three public datasets (NASA, GOTION, and TJU), covering different chemistries and capacities, demonstrate that BATTER-MoE achieves competitive performance compared with representative baselines. Across multiple settings, it achieves the best or tied-best MAE/RMSE in most evaluations while maintaining a competitive balance between predictive accuracy and computational cost. This study suggests that sparse conditional computation is a promising direction for battery prognostics when both predictive accuracy and computational efficiency are important.

title: Plotkin-like Bound and Explicit Function-Correcting Code Constructions for Lee Metric Channels
authors: K. Hareesh, N.T. Rashid Ummer, B. Sundar Rajan
abstract: Function-Correcting Codes (FCCs) are a novel class of codes designed to protect function evaluations of messages against errors while minimizing redundancy. A theoretical framework for systematic FCCs to channels matched to the Lee metric has been studied recently, which introduced function-correcting Lee distance codes (FCLCs) and also derived upper and lower bounds on their optimal redundancy. In this paper, we first propose a Plotkin-like bound for irregular Lee-distance codes. We then construct explicit FCLCs for specific classes of functions, including the Lee weight, Lee weight distribution, modular sum and locally bounded function. For these functions, lower bounds on redundancy are obtained, and our constructions are shown to be optimal in certain cases. Finally, a comparative analysis with classical Lee error-correcting codes and codes correcting errors in function values demonstrates that FCLCs can significantly reduce redundancy while preserving function correctness.

title: A PWM Frequency Harmonic Suppression Scheme for Driving Permanent Magnet Synchronous Motors Using Three-Level Inverters with Zero-Vector Switching Spread Spectrum Modulation
authors: Huidong Huang, Yongxiang Xu, Wentao Zhang, Jibin Zou
abstract: This paper presents a hybrid harmonic suppression scheme for three-level inverter-fed permanent magnet synchronous motor drives. By embedding spread-spectrum modulation into the zero-vector switching SVPWM (ZSSVPWM) framework, the proposed approach simultaneously suppresses odd- and even-order PWM harmonics. ZSSVPWM inherently eliminates harmonics at odd multiples of the carrier frequency through rearrangement of zero-vector sequences; the addition of random or periodic carrier frequency modulation further flattens the remaining harmonic peaks across the entire spectrum. Two integrated strategies—zero-vector switching random carrier frequency modulation (ZSRCFM) and zero-vector switching periodic carrier frequency modulation (ZSPCFM)—are developed and evaluated. Simulation and experimental results demonstrate that both methods significantly reduce total harmonic distortion and acoustic noise compared to conventional SVPWM and ZSSVPWM.

title: Distributed Predefined-Time Control for Islanded AC Microgrid Under FDI Attacks
authors: Houjun Liang, Bo Li, Xiaowei Jiang, Shun Xu
abstract: The emergence of false data injection (FDI) attacks seriously threatens the stable operation of the microgrid cyber-physical system (CPS). To tackle such a problem, a distributed extended state observer (ESO) is constructed by taking into account both attack signals and unmodeled dynamics, so as to suppress FDI attacks imposed on distributed generation units in AC microgrids. However, in consideration of the response speed and bandwidth requirements in the design of the state observer, there are certain observation errors for high-frequency attack signals. Given the strong robustness of sliding mode control to external disturbances and various attack signals, a predefined-time nonsingular fast terminal sliding mode control scheme is developed on the basis of consensus theory to compensate for the estimation errors of the observer. The proposed approach cannot only mitigate the adverse effects generated by FDI attacks and alleviate the chattering phenomenon inherent in conventional sliding mode control, but also realize the consensus convergence of frequency and voltage in a predefined time. Finally, a simulation platform with four distributed generators (DGs) is established in MATLAB/Simulink for validation. The outcomes demonstrate the efficacy of the presented distributed predefined-time sliding mode control strategy.

title: Nanostructured ZnO Thin Film-Based Enzymatic Biosensor for Sensitive Acetylcholine Detection in Neurological Applications
authors: Po-Hui Yang, Hong-Wei Zhu, Jung-Chuan Chou, Chih-Hsien Lai, Po-Yu Kuo, Yu-Hsun Nien, Yu-Wei Chen, Li-Yao Song, Lian-Sheng Jiang
abstract: This paper presents a potentiometric enzyme biosensor for acetylcholine based on a zinc oxide (ZnO) thin film. The sensing film is modified with acetylcholinesterase (AChE) and choline oxidase (ChOx) to enhance sensing performance. Furthermore, the sensing film utilizes the surface chemistry of metal oxides, functionalizing the ZnO thin film with (3-aminopropyl) triethoxysilane (APTES). AChE-ChOx is then immobilized on the working electrode via glutaraldehyde (GA) cross-linking. Potentiometric measurements using the time-voltage (V-T) method demonstrate a linear detection range of 10 nM to 100 μM, with a detection limit as low as 6.33 nM, reaching physiological concentrations. This sensor exhibits an average sensitivity of 48.20 mV/decade, a linearity of 0.990, a response time of 17 seconds, and excellent anti-interference performance.

title: Modeling and Compensation of LEO Ephemeris Errors in Differential Ranging Measurements for Enhanced PNT Accuracy
authors: Joe Saroufim, Zaher M. Kassas
publish: IEEE Transactions on Aerospace and Electronic Systems
abstract: Differential ranging measurements from ground based receivers to low Earth orbit (LEO) satellites with erroneous ephemerides (e.g., non-cooperative satellites) are studied and a method to compensate for differential ranging measurement errors is developed. A single-difference framework is proposed in which a base receiver with a known position communicates pseudorange measurements from overhead LEO space vehicles (SVs) with erroneous ephemerides to a rover receiver, with an unknown position. It is shown that the ephemeris error impact on the single difference measurement can be characterized in terms of one sufficient parameter: the ephemeris error magnitude, which can be estimated using differential measurements via a batch weighted nonlinear least-squares (B-WNLS) estimator. The estimated parameter is employed in a proposed differential measurement correction algorithm to accurately localize the rover receiver. The consistency of an extended Kalman filter (EKF) for receiver positioning that fuses differential pseudorange measurements is evaluated, showing that single-difference range-type measurements in the presence of ephemeris errors yield suboptimal estimator performance and large estimation bias. However, employing the proposed ephemeris error correction scheme is able to mitigate the effect of the ephemeris errors on the differential measurements. Next, the influence of the baseline orientation and distance on the differential range error is examined, showing that the ephemeris error magnitude and the baseline distance do not affect the differential residual along specific baseline orientations, which can be characterized by the angle formed between the line-of-sight vectors at both receivers. Finally, experimental results are presented where differential carrier phase measurements from 10 Starlink, 4 OneWeb, and 2 Orbcomm LEO SVs are used to localize a stationary rover receiver via a B-WNLS. Using Monte Carlo analysis with an average 3-D initial position error of 126.84 km, utilizing erroneous LEO SV ephemerides from two-line element (TLE) files, propagated with SGP4, resulted in a 3-D positioning error of 2,752 m. In contrast, using differential carrier phase measurements from a base with a known position, 6.92 km away from the rover, resulted in a 3-D positioning error of 31.3 m. However, utilizing the proposed correction method reduced the error to 5.3 m.

title: Analysis of the Abnormal Degradation Mechanism in a-IGZO Thin-Film Transistors Under Self-Heating Stress
authors: Han-Yu Chang, William Cheng-Yu Ma, Ting-Chang Chang, Chia-Chuan Wu, Hong-Yi Tu, Su-Wen Yang, Hung-I Liang, Po-Ying Yen, Tsung-Ming Tsai
abstract: In this work, self-heating stress (SHS) reveals an abnormal degradation behavior in short-channel a-InGaZnO (a-IGZO) thin-film transistors (TFTs) fabricated using a hydrogen-suppression process. Although the suppression scheme effectively maintains an appropriate threshold voltage (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">${V}_{\text {th}}$</tex-math> </inline-formula>) under initial conditions, the devices exhibit an unexpected degradation sequence during SHS: an initial positive <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">${V}_{\text {th}}$</tex-math> </inline-formula> shift, followed by an abrupt transition into a normally-<sc xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">on</small> state as stress continues. Detailed electrical characterization indicates that the early stage <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">${V}_{\text {th}}$</tex-math> </inline-formula> shift is dominated by electron trapping in the gate insulator (GI), while the subsequent sudden turn-on behavior originates from hydrogen diffusion throughout the entire channel, which alters the channel potential profile and weakens gate control. Capacitance–voltage (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$C$</tex-math> </inline-formula>–<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$V$</tex-math> </inline-formula>) measurements further confirm the presence of hydrogen migration, as evidenced by the significant increase in parasitic capacitance with stress time. Based on these observations, a physical degradation model is proposed and validated through TCAD simulations, showing strong agreement in the evolution of <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">${V}_{\text {th}}$</tex-math> </inline-formula>, subthreshold swing (S.S.) degradation, and current distribution.

title: Agentic AI for Dynamic EV Charging: Explainable Coordination of Fixed Charging Stations and Mobile Charging Vehicles
authors: Joy Dutta
abstract: Electric vehicle charging is becoming an operations problem as much as an infrastructure problem. Fixed charging stations provide essential base capacity, but they are spatially rigid and often mismatched to short-term demand surges caused by traffic, events, and local disruptions. Mobile charging vehicles add flexibility, yet coordinating fixed and mobile assets at city scale remains difficult for conventional optimization and reinforcement learning alone. This article argues that agentic AI can serve as a bounded orchestration layer above existing scheduling and control methods. We present a practical architecture in which LLM-based planning agents translate user preferences, operator goals, and policy constraints into verified tasks for lower-level schedulers and controllers managing both fixed charging stations and mobile charging vehicles. We also discuss explainability, human oversight, deployment challenges, and a lightweight simulator-based case study comparing bounded LLM-assisted coordination against deterministic baselines under routine and stress conditions. The goal is not to replace domain-specific models but to make dynamic EV charging more transparent, adaptable, and easier to align with transport, grid, and public-interest objectives.

title: An LSTM-Driven Differential Game Scheme for Formation and Configuration of Tethered Space Net Robot
authors: Junxiang Li, Fan Zhang, Yifeng Ma, Ya Liu, Panfeng Huang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Aerospace and Electronic Systems
abstract: Past research on the Tethered Space Net Robot (TSNR) was confined to satellite-based passive control due to its unique flexible structure, and consequently, the flexible net configuration in the forward direction could not be effectively managed during formation maneuvers. Besides, its entanglement with the target introduces additional challenges. To address these issues, a formation control framework is presented that utilizes neural networks and differential game theory for active control of flexible underactuated systems. A refined variable, termed the envelope depth, is proposed to characterize the 3D configuration of the flexible net. This variable establishes a critical bridge between satellite-level control and net-level manipulation. Moreover, an LSTM-MLP (Long Short-Term Memory-Multi-Layer Perceptron) neural network is applied to capture temporal features. It learns the nonlinear dynamic model of the envelope depth. Thereby, a coupling relationship between the flexible-net motion and the satellite dynamics is established. Furthermore, a neural game-theoretic formation configuration controller (NGT-FCC) based on differential game theory is developed to achieve cooperative control of satellite formation and 3D configuration. The effectiveness of proposed controller is demonstrated by numerical simulation results. The results show that, by actively controlling the envelope depth, it effectively suppresses the disturbance and limits the oscillation, reducing the envelope depth amplitude by 82.89%.

title: A Shared-Leg NPC (SLNPC) Converter for HV Three Phase Switched Reluctance Motor Drives
authors: Ali Yousefi-Darani, Mehdi Narimani
publish: IEEE Open Journal of Power Electronics
abstract: This paper presents a novel and cost-effective multilevel inverter design for three-phase switched reluctance motor (SRM) drives. The proposed inverter employs a three-level topology with a shared-leg neutral point diode clamped (SLNPC) circuit, which utilizes two shared legs for adjacent phases. This innovative approach significantly reduces the size and cost of the drive by minimizing the number of required components, including power switches, diodes, gate drivers, and current sensors. Despite the reduction in components, the inverter delivers performance comparable to conventional three-level asymmetric NPC topology. Independent operating modes and common operating modes between adjacent phases are discussed in detail. The effectiveness of the three-level SLNPC topology is validated through comprehensive simulation studies and experimental results on a SRM drive system.

title: Nontrivial Effect of Ni Layer on Intermetallic Compounds Growth in Lead-Free Microbumps Under Coupled Thermal and Current Stress
authors: Zhiwei Fu, Hao Yu, Chenbing Qu, Linhua Liu, Xiao Luo, Jia-Yue Yang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Components, Packaging and Manufacturing Technology
abstract: Interfacial intermetallic compound (IMC) reactions determine the reliability of lead-free microbump interconnects. A Ni barrier layer can suppress these reactions, but its suppression mechanism is still not fully understood. This work studies the Ni barrier layer function under coupled thermal and current stress. We analyze Cu and Ni atom migration and build a kinetic model for IMC evolution that can be applied to predict the thickness growth of IMC layers before complete solder consumption. The results reveal that electromigration accelerates the dissolution of the cathodic Ni layer, thereby enabling direct reactions between Cu pillar and solder. The kinetic model shows that IMC growth rates follow the order: anodic Cu6Sn5 > anodic Cu3Sn ≈ cathodic Cu3Sn > cathodic Cu6Sn5 > anodic (Cu1-xNiₓ)6Sn5 ≈ cathodic (Cu1-xNiₓ)6Sn5. Under current stress, the dominant effect is the acceleration of rapid complete alloying, mainly through enhanced growth of anodic Cu6Sn5. In addition, Joule heating strengthens both electromigration- and thermally driven IMC growth. This shortens the time for complete solder consumption. Combined with experimental analysis, it is found that after solder consumption, as the Ni atomic fraction in (Cu1-xNiₓ)6Sn5 decreases, the evolution of (Cu1-xNiₓ)6Sn5 and the Ni layer accelerates significantly. Nevertheless, the limited duration following solder consumption allows the anodic Ni layer to preserve a substantial thickness and maintain its structural integrity. This polarity-dependent consumption has a nontrivial impact on the long-term reliability of microbump interconnects.

title: Area-Oriented Threshold Logic Circuit Synthesis Using Negative Weights
authors: Yu-Chuan Yen, Fu-Cheng Cai, Yi-Ting Li, Wuqian Tang, Yung-Chih Chen, Ihao Chen, Chun-Yao Wang
publish: 2026 27th International Symposium on Quality Electronic Design (ISQED)
abstract: This paper presents a novel approach to Threshold Logic Network (TLN) synthesis, which effectively leverages negative weights to minimize area costs. Traditional synthesis methods predominantly use positive weights, which limits their potential in certain hardware implementations. Our approach reduces the area cost of threshold logic gates without sacrificing circuit expressiveness. Using the positive-negative weight transformation method, we achieve significant area reductions while maintaining the expressive capabilities of the TLN. Experimental results demonstrate our approach’s consistent benefits, achieving reduction ratio up to 11.97% in modern threshold logic circuit design, thus showcasing its effectiveness.

title: A Mixed-Signal Neuromorphic Accelerator for Energy Efficient Inference in Event-Based Neural Network Models
authors: Armin Abdollahi, Mehdi Kamal, Massoud Pedram
publish: 2026 27th International Symposium on Quality Electronic Design (ISQED)
abstract: This paper introduces a comprehensive mixed-signal neuromorphic accelerator architecture to enhance inference in event-based neural network models. The accelerator is fully compatible with CMOS technology and employs analog computing techniques to replicate synaptic and neuronal operations. A C2C ladder structure implements synapses, while operational amplifiers handle neuronal functions. To optimize hardware resource utilization and power efficiency, we propose the concept of a virtual neuron. This allows a single-neuron engine to simulate a set of model neurons by capitalizing on the sparsity characteristic of event-based neuromorphic systems. Additionally, we present a memory-based control technique to effectively manage events within each network layer, thereby improving performance while offering flexibility to accommodate various types of layers. Furthermore, we introduce a novel integer linear programming (ILP)–based mapping approach for efficiently allocating the model onto the proposed accelerator. The architecture is evaluated using two event-based datasets demonstrating its scalability, including the large and complex CIFAR10-DVS. Despite the inherent complexities associated with the dataset, the architecture achieves an impressive energy efficiency of 12.1 TOPS/W. Our results show that our design maintains high accuracy using a markedly lower number of physical neurons, while the chip area—even when implemented in a $90-\mathrm{nm}$ process—is smaller than that of previous implementations. Moreover, as the dataset size increases, the achievable TOPS/W performance further improves, underscoring the scalability of our approach.

title: Crossing the Layers and Dotting the Details: Systematic Exploration of Near-Memory Computing
authors: Riselda Kodra, Rafael Medina, Marina Zapater, Giovanni Ansaloni, David Atienza
publish: 2026 27th International Symposium on Quality Electronic Design (ISQED)
abstract: Near-Memory Computing (NMC) addresses the Von Neumann bottleneck between memory and processing by bringing computation capabilities close to where data is stored. This approach opens a vast design space, which requires both a detailed view of the NMC processing units (PUs) architecture and a broad perspective of the entire system integrating them. To tackle this challenge, this paper presents a novel cross-layer methodology that relies on synthesizable hardware models of the NMC PUs, encapsulated as components for event-based fullsystem simulation. Our approach enables accurate assessment of the resource requirements and efficiency of near-memory units while exploring them in context, i.e., when integrated into a system executing complex workloads. Hence, the resulting framework facilitates the systematic design exploration of NMC solutions. To this end, it also includes a complete software stack that supports the implementation and benchmarking of NMC-accelerated applications. We illustrate its effectiveness by providing area, energy, and performance metrics for executing Machine Learning (ML) inference benchmarks across the host CPU and the PUs using different DRAM standards. Our cross-layer approach highlights the potential of NMC acceleration, demonstrating system-wide speedups of $17.4 \times$ on average.

title: Errata to “A 19-dBm 110–142-GHz CMOS Power Amplifier With Device-Centric Power Boosting and Dual-Coupled Slotline Combiner”
authors: Md Hedayatullah Maktoomi, Xuyang Liu, Hamidreza Aghasi
abstract: In the above article [1], which is an expanded version of [2], the authors would like to clarify that a drain supply voltage of $V_{DD} ~=2.6$ V was applied to all three stages during the measurements. In addition, measurements performed on multiple chip samples, wire-bonded on different PCB boards, indicate that in certain cases the gain exceeds the 3-dB threshold within the 110–116-GHz frequency range. To ensure a more consistent and conservative definition of bandwidth, the authors propose to revise the reported 3-dB bandwidth to 26.5 GHz (116.3–142.8 GHz) with a small-signal gain of 14.9 dB in Table II. Accordingly, the figures of merit FoMA, FoMB, and FoMC will be updated to 17.52, 10.65, and 29.56, respectively.

title: Proteus: A Morpheus II Extension for System-Level Moving-Target Defense and Key-Exposure Hardening
authors: Jeremy Knickerbocker, Syed Rafay Hasan, Amr Hilal, Mohammad Ashiqur Rahman
publish: 2026 27th International Symposium on Quality Electronic Design (ISQED)
abstract: Lightweight block ciphers, such as SIMON, are widely used in embedded and Internet of Things (IoT) systems that must balance security with limited hardware and energy budgets. The Morpheus II processor employs a $\mathbf{1 2}$-round SIMON core to protect architectural metadata and support moving-target defense (MTD). However, the core encrypts only at startup or upon fault detection, leaving long intervals where ciphertext in memory remains static. In Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition (SCADA)-connected IoT devices, adversaries with read and write access to main memory can exploit these intervals to analyze or manipulate encrypted state, leaving Morpheus II vulnerable to prolonged observation and control-flow manipulation. This work modifies the Morpheus II encryption core to reintroduce proactive churn, making Morpheus II suitable for IoT environments. The modified core, called Proteus, retains SIMON’s Feistel structure and key schedule but replaces its fixed round function with predefined rotation sets selected by a system-controlled perepoch schedule. Rotation sets can be mapped to memory regions, enabling cipher-level MTD. Initial cryptanalysis evaluation shows configuration-dependent improvements in differential behavior relative to the 12 -round SIMON baseline, at a modest cost of area and performance.

title: Security-Quality Scorecard: A Comprehensive Framework for Quantitative Evaluation of Hardware-Enforced Boot Chain Security
authors: Hyunmin Kim
publish: 2026 27th International Symposium on Quality Electronic Design (ISQED)
abstract: Modern embedded systems face increasingly sophisticated attacks targeting boot chain vulnerabilities, necessitating rigorous quantitative security evaluation frameworks. Existing approaches lack comprehensive metrics, standardized evaluation methodologies, and hardware-accelerated collection mechanisms with formal completeness guarantees. We present the SecurityQuality Scorecard (SQS), a novel framework that integrates six complementary, low-correlation security metrics—systematically derived from the STRIDE threat taxonomy—with formal verification using Z3 SMT solver for comprehensive evaluation of boot chains. Our framework introduces: (1) mathematically rigorous Formal Coverage Score (FCS) quantifying test completeness via satisfiability modulo theories, (2) hardware/software co-design for real-time metric collection via custom RTL modules, and (3) probabilistic security testing across normal operation and attack scenarios. Through extensive hardware measurement on Zynq-7000 SoC across $\mathbf{7 5, 0 0 0}$ test samples (three independent replications, 25,000 each) covering 10 security situations, we demonstrate $\mathbf{9 6 \%}$ formal coverage score with $\mathbf{1 0 0 \%}$ state coverage (11/11 states) and 100% transition coverage (33/33 transitions). Probabilistic attack simulation achieves a Boot Integrity Score (BIS) of $\mathbf{4 2. 6 6 \%} \pm \mathbf{0. 6 7 \%}(\mathbf{C V}=\mathbf{1. 5 8 \%})$, validating both functional correctness ($10,666 \pm 168$ successes) and security resilience (zero bypasses in attack scenarios). Three independent hardware replications demonstrate reproducible measurement with natural statistical variation, confirming the reliability of the framework. The hardware acceleration enables cycle-accurate metric collection with minimal overhead. Our implementation facilitates reproducible security evaluation and standardization across heterogeneous embedded platforms.

title: BPINN-EM-Post: Bayesian Physics-Informed Neural Network based Stochastic Electromigration Damage Analysis in the Post-void Phase
authors: Subed Lamichhane, Haotian Lu, Sheldon X.-D. Tan
publish: 2026 27th International Symposium on Quality Electronic Design (ISQED)
abstract: Electromigration (EM)-induced stress evolution is inherently non-deterministic due to input current fluctuations and manufacturing non-idealities. Existing approaches for estimating stress variations rely on computationally expensive Monte Carlo simulations with industrial solvers. This work presents BPINN-EM-Post, a framework for stochastic analysis of EMinduced post-voiding aging processes. The key contributions are: (1) We integrate closed-form analytical solutions with a Bayesian Physics-Informed Neural Network (BPINN) to accelerate stochastic EM analysis. The closed-form solutions enforce physical laws at individual wire segments, while BPINN satisfies physics constraints at inter-segment junctions and captures stochastic behaviors. (2) By reducing loss function variables through analytical solutions, training efficiency improves substantially without sacrificing accuracy, and variational effects are naturally incorporated. (3) The analytical solutions address the challenge of incorporating initial stress distributions during post-void stress calculations. Experimental results show that BPINN-EMPost achieves over $240 \times$ and $67 \times$ speedup compared to Monte Carlo simulations using FEM-based COMSOL and FDM-based EMSpice, respectively, with marginal accuracy loss.

title: VeriRAG: A Retrieval-Augmented Framework for Automated RTL Testability Repair
authors: Haomin Qi, Yuyang Du, Lihao Zhang, Soung Chang Liew, Kexin Chen, Yining Du
publish: 2026 27th International Symposium on Quality Electronic Design (ISQED)
abstract: Large language models (LLMs) have demonstrated immense potential in computer-aided design (CAD), particularly for automated debugging and verification within electronic design automation (EDA) tools. However, Design for Testability (DFT) remains a relatively underexplored area. This paper presents VeriRAG, the first LLM-assisted DFT-EDA framework. VeriRAG leverages a Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) approach to enable LLM to revise code to ensure DFT compliance. VeriRAG integrates (1) an autoencoder-based similarity measurement model for precise retrieval of reference RTL designs for the LLM, and (2) an iterative code revision pipeline that allows the LLM to ensure DFT compliance while maintaining synthesizability. To support VeriRAG, we introduce VeriDFT, a Verilog-based DFT dataset curated for DFT-aware RTL repairs. VeriRAG retrieves structurally similar RTL designs from VeriDFT, each paired with a rigorously validated correction, as references for code repair. With VeriRAG and VeriDFT, we achieve fully automated DFT correction - resulting in a 7.72-fold improvement in successful repair rate compared to the zeroshot baseline (Fig. 5 in Section V). Ablation studies further confirm the contribution of each component of the VeriRAG framework. We open-source our data, models, and scripts at https://github.com/yuyangdu01/LLM4DFT.

title: Shadow Integrity Control (sic)
authors: Amirreza Hashemi, Hans Liljestrand, Carlos Chinea Perez, Jan-Erik Ekberg
publish: 2026 27th International Symposium on Quality Electronic Design (ISQED)
abstract: Control-flow integrity (CFI) is an active area of research that includes a wealth of software and hardware-based solutions. Nevertheless, in practice only a handful of solutions have been deployed, typically only in limited scenarios. In this paper, we present SIC, a hardware-assisted CFI solution utilizing recent ARM instruction set architecture (ISA) extensions that provide a shadow stack and coarse-grained CFI. We combine these in a novel way to achieve a fine-grained forward-edge CFI that is compatible with memory protection features such as code randomization and execute-only memory (XOM) and has an overhead of 1.7% based on SPEC CPU 2017 benchmarks.

title: Data Augmentation Strategies for Machine Learning-based Compact Modeling of Emerging Devices
authors: Diego Ferrer, Md. Mazharul Islam, Wei Pan, Juan P. Mendez, Denis Malamuy, Ahmedullah Aziz
publish: 2026 27th International Symposium on Quality Electronic Design (ISQED)
abstract: In this paper, we demonstrate two novel data augmentation methods designed to enable the training of a ML-based compact model for an emerging device with extremely limited experimental data. These methods address sparse data and class imbalance, some of the most difficult challenges faced when trying to create a ML-based compact model. In this work, we apply these methods in the creation of an ML-based compact model for the quantum-enhanced Josephson Junction Field Effect Transistor, an emerging quantum-enhanced device with substantial potential but scarce experimental characterization. To account for this, we developed Dynamic Gaussian Noise Data Augmentation (DGNDA), a method of generating additional data points using those already available, and Experimental and Simulation Data Mixing (ESDM), a method of using simulation data to augment the experimental data used for training. DGNDA demonstrated strengthened accuracy in modeling underrepresented classes and ESDM maintained the accuracy of the model comparable to training exclusively on experimental data.

title: Satellite Microwave Links for Opportunistic Sensing of Volcanic Plumes: A Case Study on Mt. Etna
authors: Emanuele M. Sciortino, Fabiola Sapienza, Ottavia Gherardini, Filippo Giannetti, Alessandro Piras, Vincenzo Lottici
publish: IEEE Open Journal of Antennas and Propagation
abstract: Volcanic plumes can significantly affect microwave propagation, especially in the Ku- and Ka-bands, where ash particles and water vapor emissions introduce measurable attenuation. This work investigates the use of geostationary satellite downlinks as opportunistic sensing resources for monitoring volcanic activity. Focusing on Mt. Etna as a representative testbed, the study characterizes the impact of volcanic components on microwave signals, models ash dispersion through a gaussian model, and evaluates the resulting attenuation along realistic geostationary link geometries. A key objective is to identify an optimal site on the volcano for installing a dedicated ground terminal capable of continuously detecting plume-induced impairments, taking into account geometrical visibility, dominant wind patterns, and practical logistical constraints. The results highlight the potential of geostationary downlinks as a complementary, low-cost tool for volcanic plume monitoring and provide a framework for the deployment of an operational opportunistic sensing station.

title: Edge-Guided Spatial and Frequency Synergistic Perception Network for Remote Sensing Image Semantic Segmentation
authors: Pange Shang, Shuli Cheng, Anyu Du
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: Semantic segmentation of high-resolution remote sensing imagery plays a fundamental role in land-cover mapping and urban analysis. Unlike natural images, remote sensing scenes often exhibit pronounced cross-scale structural heterogeneity: roads usually appear as elongated and topologically continuous targets, building regions present compact yet complex geometric layouts, and agricultural areas tend to form large contiguous pat terns. However, existing segmentation networks mainly empha size pixel-level recognition and generic multi-scale fusion, which often leads to structural fragmentation, boundary discontinuity, and semantic-detail misalignment in remote sensing imagery. To address these issues, we propose an Edge-Guided Spatial and Frequency Synergistic Perception Network (ESFSPNet), a structure-aware segmentation framework designed to preserve geospatial continuity and boundary integrity in complex remote sensing scenes. Specifically, we introduce a Spatial-Frequency Structural Interaction (SFSI) module to jointly model local structural details and global morphological patterns from spatial and frequency perspectives. Within SFSI, the Spatial Multi Scale Kernel Modulation (SMKM) branch adaptively captures heterogeneous local structures, while the Frequency-Domain Enhancement (FDE) branch strengthens long-range structural dependencies and global consistency. Furthermore, an Edge Guided Cross-Attention Fusion (ECAF) module is developed in the decoder to align high-level semantics with low-level structural cues during upsampling, thereby alleviating object breakage and boundary drift. Extensive experiments on the ISPRS Vaihingen, ISPRS Potsdam, and LoveDA datasets show that ESFSPNet achieves more competitive performance than existing methods, particularly in maintaining structural integrity and boundary consistency of remote sensing targets.

title: Performance, Power, and Limit-of-Detection Tradeoffs across Gate- and Source-Engineered TFET Biosensors for HER2 Detection Saliva and Serum
authors: R Priyadharshni, K Vijaya Kumar, V Parvathy, Aniruddha Kanhe, Suresh Balanethiram
publish: IEEE Journal of the Electron Devices Society
abstract: Early-stage breast cancer diagnosis requires biosensors that are not only highly sensitive but also energy efficient and capable of detecting ultra-low HER2 (C-erbB-2) biomarker concentrations in saliva and serum, enabling reliable early-stage screening. Since biosensor sensitivity strongly depends on the subthreshold behaviour of the sensing transistor, Tunnel Field Effect Transistors (TFETs) are attractive due to their inherently steep switching enabled by band-to-band tunneling. While gate engineering through double-gate (DG) and triple-gate (TG) architectures improves electrostatic control and source heterojunction (HJ) engineering enhances tunneling efficiency, their combined impact on practical biosensing performance must be evaluated across a broader figure-of-merit space beyond sensitivity alone. To address this, a comparative simulation study of gate-engineered DG-TFET, TG-TFET, and gate- and source-engineered HJ-TG-TFET biosensors was carried out for C-erbB-2 detection in clinically relevant saliva and serum concentration ranges. The devices are evaluated using a broad biosensor performance framework, including sensitivity, power dissipation, limit of detection, detection margin, steric-hindrance tolerance, and signal-to-noise ratio. The results show that while gate engineering improves sensitivity, the combined gate- and source-engineered HJ-TG-TFET proposed in this work achieves the best overall trade-off, simultaneously delivering better sensitivity, lower power dissipation, superior low-concentration detectability for early-stage diagnosis, improved detection margin, and stronger robustness against steric hindrance. These findings establish HJ-TG-TFET as a promising energy-efficient biosensor for early-stage C-erbB-2 breast cancer biomarker detection and demonstrate that combined gate and source engineering is a highly effective pathway to improve practical TFET biosensor performance.

title: Bhasha-Rupantarika: Algorithm-Hardware Co-design approach for Multilingual Neural Machine Translation
authors: Mukul Lokhande, Tanushree Dewangan, Mohd Sharik Mansoor, Tejas Chaudhari, Akarsh J, Damayanti Lokhande, Adam Teman, Santosh Kumar Vishvakarma
publish: 2026 27th International Symposium on Quality Electronic Design (ISQED)
abstract: This paper introduces Bhasha-Rupantarika, a light and efficient multilingual translation system tailored through algorithm-hardware codesign for resource-limited settings. The method investigates model deployment at sub-octet precision levels (FP8, INT8, INT4, and FP4), with experimental results indicating a $4.1 \times$ reduction in model size (FP4) and a $4.2 \times$ speedup in inference, correlating with a 66 tokens/s increase in throughput $(4.8 \times)$. This underscores the importance of ultra-low precision quantization for real-time deployment in IoT devices using FPGA accelerators, achieving performance on par with expectations. Our evaluation covers bidirectional translation between Indian and international languages, showcasing its adaptability in lowresource linguistic contexts. The FPGA deployment demonstrated a $1.96 \times$ reduction in LUTs and a $1.65 \times$ decrease in FFs, resulting in a $2.2 \times$ increase in throughput compared to OPU and a $4.6 \times$ increase compared to HPTA. Overall, the evaluation provides a viable solution based on quantisation-aware translation along with hardware efficiency suitable for deployable multilingual AI systems. The entire code and dataset for reproducibility are publicly available at https://github.com/mukullokhande99/BhashaRupantarika/ facilitating rapid integration and further development by researchers.

title: CRISP: Platform-Agnostic Unified Reconfigurable Hardware Security Primitive
authors: Atri Chatterjee, Sudipta Paria, Aritra Dasgupta, Habibur Rahaman, Baibhab Chatterjee, Swarup Bhunia
publish: 2026 27th International Symposium on Quality Electronic Design (ISQED)
abstract: Modern digital systems face diverse security threats, such as piracy, counterfeiting, and side-channel attacks, across different stages of the IC lifecycle. Addressing these threats individually increases design complexity, cost, and overhead, often forcing trade-offs against other critical design parameters. This paper presents CRISP, a unified, reconfigurable, and platformagnostic infrastructure for hardware security primitives that consolidates three key features: (i) static entropy via a Physical Unclonable Function (PUF), (ii) dynamic entropy via a True Random Number Generator (TRNG), and (iii) secure access to scan-based Design-for-Test (DFT) into a single reconfigurable circuit-level fabric. CRISP integrates these security features within the augmented flip-flops, enabling distributed and flexible access to these primitives. The all-digital nature of CRISP primitives makes it amenable to automation and attractive for both ASIC and FPGA platforms. Experimental evaluation shows promising results with PUF achieving near-ideal inter-HD ($49.45 \%$) and Pearson Correlation Coefficient (-0.01) with Bit Error Rate (BER) $\leq 20 \%$, while TRNG shows autocorrelation function (ACF) with 95\% confidence and both pass all the NIST SP800-22 Randomness Tests. Fabricated on a 65 nm test chip, CRISP demonstrates low area and power overheads of $\mathbf{0 . 0 7 1} \times \mathbf{1 0}^{\mathbf{6}} \mathbf{F}^{\mathbf{2}} \boldsymbol{/}$ bit and $\mathbf{1 1 . 8 2} \mathbf{f J} \boldsymbol{/} \mathbf{b i t}$, respectively, demonstrating its practical viability.

title:
Thermal-Aware Compact Modeling and Design-Space Exploration of δ-Doped β-Ga
                    <sub>2</sub>
                    O
                    <sub>3</sub>
                    MOSFETs for RF Power Electronics
authors: Habibullah Khan, Nabasindhu Das, Nidhin Kurian Kalarickal, Kexin Li
publish: 2026 27th International Symposium on Quality Electronic Design (ISQED)
abstract: β-Ga<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf>O<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</inf> is an ultrawide-bandgap semiconductor with exceptional breakdown strength, high critical electric field, and scalability, making it a promising material for nextgeneration power and RF electronics. However, its intrinsically low thermal conductivity and strong self-heating effects remain major challenges to reliable operation. This work develops a physics-based compact model for $\beta-\mathrm{Ga}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$ MOSFETs with deltadoped channel that is suitable for circuit-level simulation and design exploration. The model is calibrated using experimentally validated TCAD simulation results and captures key physical phenomena, including quantum confinement, electron centroid shift under bias, self-heating, and parasitic resistances. With a small set of physically meaningful parameters, the proposed model achieves geometry scalability and enables predictive simulation across a wide design space. Comprehensive validation against both TCAD and experimental data demonstrates excellent agreement for current-voltage (I-V) characteristics. The developed model provides a compact yet accurate framework for understanding electrothermal interactions in $\beta-\mathrm{Ga}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$ devices and supports circuit designers in optimizing performance, efficiency, and reliability.

title: Uncertainty-Guided Adaptive Model Hopping for Efficient Object Tracking on Edge Devices
authors: Divake Kumar, Amanda Rios, Nilesh Ahuja, Omesh Tickoo, Amit Ranjan Trivedi
publish: 2026 27th International Symposium on Quality Electronic Design (ISQED)
abstract: Fixed-model deployment in object detection wastes computation on easy frames and degrades performance on challenging ones. We present an uncertainty-guided adaptive model hopping system that dynamically switches among five YOLOv8 models (3.2M-68.2M parameters) based on per-frame tracking difficulty. Unlike prior approaches that rely on systemlevel indicators such as CPU load or bandwidth, the method uses detector confidence and lightweight uncertainty signals to estimate frame complexity and allocate compute accordingly. High-confidence frames are processed with lightweight models, while low-confidence or high-uncertainty detections trigger transitions to higher-capacity models. Bidirectional switching with hysteresis prevents oscillation and supports escalation under challenging conditions as well as de-escalation when confidence recovers. Experiments on seven MOT17 sequences (4,746 frames) demonstrate a 58.2% reduction in computation relative to always using YOLOv8-XLarge while preserving $99.6 \%$ tracking success. The system achieves 28 FPS on an Intel NUC 14 Pro using OpenVINO, demonstrating practical real-time edge deployment without model retraining or architectural modification.

title: Unsupervised Detection of Ring-Oscillator Hardware Trojans via Autoencoder Power Analysis
authors: Oyshi Sarkar, Jaya Dofe
publish: 2026 27th International Symposium on Quality Electronic Design (ISQED)
abstract: This work presents an unsupervised method for detecting ring-oscillator hardware Trojans in AES-128 FPGA implementations using side-channel power traces. The evaluated Trojan inserts an 11-stage LUT-based ring oscillator activated by an internal AES subnode, resulting in subtle but measurable deviations in switching activity. A dataset of 40,000 traces—20,000 clean and 20,000 Trojan across GF and LUT S-Boxes was analyzed using a deep autoencoder trained solely on clean data. Trojan traces exhibit significantly higher reconstruction error, enabling threshold-based classification. Results achieve $84.3 \%$ accuracy with clear separation between clean and Trojan samples, demonstrating the effectiveness of unsupervised reconstruction-error analysis for scalable Trojan detection.

title: Towards intuitive and safe physical human-robot collaboration: an uninstrumented touchpad interface powered by intrinsic robot tactile sensing_supp2-3695745.mp4
authors: Pedro Neto
abstract: Collaborative robots are increasingly prevalent in both industrial and domestic settings, demanding intuitive, safe and user-friendly interfaces for effective physical human-robot interaction (pHRI). External tactile sensors or buttons can enhance pHRI capabilities, but they often introduce additional complexity and cost. To address this challenge, we propose a novel framework that leverages the intrinsic tactile sensing capabilities derived from joint torque sensors integrated into commercially available collaborative robots. This framework powers an uninstrumented 3D-printed touchpad, mounted on the robot’s flange, where user touches are interpreted as virtual button commands by analyzing the resulting joint torque signals. Owing to their spatio-temporal nature, touch patterns are segmented using a windowing-based process and classified using deep learning models. A dynamic hierarchical finite state machine (HFSM) manages the interactive process, ensuring intuitive human-inthe- loop interaction and safety. A dedicated safety layer mitigates potential button touch misclassifications using safety validation protocols. A novel open dataset, pHRI-BTN-TACT, containing touch data from multiple participants, is proposed. Classification results validate the system’s ability to generalize, achieving userindependent performance with an online accuracy exceeding 93% across the robot workspace. The practical viability of the framework is demonstrated through three use cases on collaborative assembly. Demonstration videos and datasets at <uri xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">https://ts-robotics.github.io/pHRI-BTN/</uri>.

title: From Signals to Semantics: A Multilabel Power Quality Disturbance Identification Framework Using Large Language Models
authors: Lixian Shi, Qiushi Cui, Yigong Zhang, Lida Shi, Hao Wu, Wenyuan Li
abstract: Accurate identification of power quality disturbances (PQDs) under the complex dynamic variations introduced by high renewable energy penetration remains a critical challenge. To address this issue, this article proposes a large language model-empowered power quality disturbance identification (LLME-PQDI) framework that converts voltage signals into semantics. To bridge voltage signals and large language model (LLM)-processable texts, a PQD semantic embedding module is developed. Within this process, a sample entropy-weighted patch contrastive learning method is introduced to strengthen feature extraction by focusing on uncertain informative signal segments. Furthermore, to enhance the LLM’s understanding of disturbance characteristics, a disturbance-guided prompt generation (DGPG) strategy is established, incorporating key time-frequency domain features of different PQD categories. This semantic conversion essentially facilitates the application of the powerful reasoning and generalization capabilities of LLMs for robust identification against distributional shifts in disturbance features. Experimental results on complex datasets with expanded parameter ranges and severe noise demonstrate that LLME-PQDI outperforms state-of-the-art approaches, achieving an exceptional accuracy of 98.05%, thereby validating the effectiveness of the proposed identification framework.

title: A Unified Framework for Reliable Survival Analysis_supp1-3697686.pdf
authors: Mohamed Bouguessa
abstract: Survival analysis (time-to-event prediction) focuses on predicting the time until an event of interest, such as disease relapse or death. It is a critical tool for clinical decisionmaking. An effective survival approach must address model understandability, quantifying uncertainty, and predictive robustness for practical adoption and trustworthiness in clinical settings. While progress has been made in these areas, existing methods often address these aspects separately, leading to limited performance and practical challenges. In this paper, we propose a principled framework that addresses these limitations in a unified approach. Our framework incorporates a stochastic, biologically inspired sparsity mechanism for feature selection and applies a Bayesian treatment of model weights, enabling double stochasticity through two distinct sampling processes. These mechanisms enhance model understandability and enable robust uncertainty quantification. Furthermore, we introduce an information-bound regularization inspired by information theory, which controls information flow by promoting the learning of essential information while filtering out irrelevant and redundant data. This regularization improves generalization and enhances predictive resilience to noise and adversarial perturbations. Through extensive experiments on five real-world survival datasets, our approach demonstrates competitive performance in both discrimination and calibration compared to state-of-the-art deep survival models. On a synthetic dataset, our approach effectively identifies relevant features, showcasing its ability to support reliable feature selection. Additionally, the robustness of the proposed framework to adversarial attacks highlights its predictive resilience, further reinforcing its trustworthiness for survival analysis applications.

title: A Feynman-Sprinkler-Like Electromagnetic Wind Energy Harvester for High-Speed Wind Fields_supp1-3695821.mp4
authors: Biao WANG
abstract: Distributed Internet of Things (IoT) nodes powered by wind energy harvesters (WEHs) have enabled long-term, maintenance-free environmental monitoring in remote and extreme scenarios. However, the large fluctuations in wind speed may cause structural fatigue and dynamical instability of conventional WEHs. To achieve stable and durable operation under high wind speeds (up to 25 m/s), this study reports a Feynman-sprinkler-like WEH (FSL-WEH). In contrast to conventional WEHs that rely on turbine blades directly driven by the wind, the FSL-WEH harnesses the reaction force generated by airflow expelled through Feynman nozzles to produce rotational motion. Simulation and experiments indicate that the FSL-WEH achieves a maximum power output of 4.34 W and a peak power density of 66.77 kW/m<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</sup> at a wind speed of 25 m/s. Moreover, the FSL-WEH successfully powers an environmental monitoring system, which wirelessly transmits temperature and humidity data to connected terminals, validating the operational reliability and durability of the device in extremely high wind speeds and high-humidity conditions. This study provides a promising solution for scavenging wind energy to power distributed environmental sensor networks under high-wind, high-humidity conditions.

title: Advanced AMSR2 Radiometer TC-Wind Algorithm for Mitigation of Rain Impact
authors: Lucrezia Ricciardulli, Thomas Meissner, Andrew Manaster
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: Here we present a new wind algorithm developed to mitigate the impact of rain and deep clouds on surface wind retrievals in Tropical Cyclones (TC) from microwave (MW) radiometers. The new algorithm has been specifically developed for the AMSR2 radiometer, but can be used for other MW sensors with similar frequency channels. Unlike other available algorithms that use mostly C/X-band channels to discriminate the wind and rain signals, the new algorithm uses all available frequency channels between 6-89 GHz (C to W-band). The algorithm is a statistical linear regression of the AMSR2 Brightness Temperatures (TB) for all 14 channels (V- and H-pol), and it has been trained using a large set of TC wind fields from the Hurricane Weather Research Forecast model (HWRF) as ground truth. The HWRF wind fields from about 200 storm scenes were interpolated and resampled to match AMSR2 time of observation and spatial resolution, and colocated with the AMSR2 TB. The new AMSR2 algorithm has been implemented as the AMSR2 TC-winds V8.2 in November 2024, and is now processed in Near Real Time (NRT) at Remote Sensing System (RSS). The performance of the new TC-winds V8.2 and has been verified with an extensive HWRF testing dataset and compared to the previous version of the algorithm V8.1. It shows a significant mitigation of the rain impact on satellite wind retrievals in TC compared to V8.1 and more accurate estimates of storm parameters (i.e. intensity and size) compared to operational products. Including the 89 GHz channels turns out to be very valuable for the mitigation of rain/deep cloud impact on wind retrievals in TC.

title: Transfer Learning for PMSM Multinode Temperature Estimation Using a Shared-Node Thermal Neural Network With Extend-Node Residuals
authors: Kai Peng, Jun Yang, Zirui Liu, Kaiyi Wang, Jian Ge, Wubin Kong
abstract: Accurate multinode temperature estimation is crucial for the safe operation of permanent magnet synchronous machines (PMSMs) in more-electric aircraft (MEA) applications. Existing methods rely on large amounts of training data and suffer from poor transferability across PMSMs with different materials. To bridge this gap, this article proposes a shared-node thermal neural network with extend-node residuals (STNN-ERs). STNN captures the shared thermal characteristics of different PMSMs. Shared nodes are selected by a proposed nearest-neighbor assignment method based on similarity analysis. To extend temperature estimation from shared nodes to multiple nodes of a specific PMSM, ERs are introduced. Small networks of ERs are used to compensate for the thermal differences between multiple nodes and shared nodes. Based on the STNN-ERs architecture, transfer learning (TL) across different PMSMs is applied. The proposed method significantly reduces the required training dataset size while achieving improved estimation accuracy. Experimental results show that STNN with TL reduces training data by 60%. Moreover, the proposed method achieves high accuracy across multiple nodes, with a maximum mean square error of 0.68 °C2 and a maximum absolute error of 2.8 °C under 16.5-h dataset.

title: Design and Simulation of Long-Retention Capacitorless 1T-DRAM Using Experimentally Calibrated Process and Device Simulations
authors: Shao-Han Cheng, Chun-Hung Wang, Fu-Chang Hsu, Yao-Jen Lee, Yiming Li
publish: 2026 27th International Symposium on Quality Electronic Design (ISQED)
abstract: In this work, a capacitorless one-transistor dynamic random-access memory (1T-DRAM) cell is investigated using calibrated process and device simulations. The simulation framework incorporates both fabrication process modeling and electrical read/write operation analysis of the floating-body storage mechanism. By optimizing the polysilicon body thickness, the device achieves its best performance at $\mathrm{T}_{\text {poly }}=20 \mathrm{~nm}$, exhibiting a sensing window of $14.48 \mu \mathrm{~A} / \mu \mathrm{m}$ and a retention time of 781 ms, representing a $10.9 \%$ improvement over the highest reported retention time for 1T-DRAM devices.

title: Cognitive-Aware Urban Navigation: IoMT-Enabled Adaptive Routing in Smart Cities
authors: Abd-Elhamid M. Taha
abstract: Significant progress has been achieved in urban navigation, including commercial support for greener commutes and emerging innovations for happiness-driven navigation. However, current systems seldom adapt to the navigator’s cognitive state, an important factor for many users and a key element of inclusive design. This article introduces a novel architecture for cognitive-aware urban navigation that combines Internet of Medical Things (IoMT) sensing with smart-city context data. The proposed layered architecture allows for closed-loop, low-latency, and privacy-conscious adaptation of routing decisions based on the traveler’s inferred cognitive state. Application scenarios include adaptive commuting, crowd-aware event navigation, and support for neurodivergent travelers. The article concludes by discussing deployment challenges and outlining a research agenda to promote more human-centered mobility in future cities.
